blocks.x: Geometry begins with the plainest things you can imagine. A single point. Two points joined make a segment, stretch it one way and you get a ray, stretch it both ways and you get a line. Now let two rays start from the same point. They open up, and the space between them is an angle. In this lesson you will learn to name angles and to measure how far they open, counted in degrees.

Angles, names, and degrees. Let two rays start at the same point and the opening between them is an angle. That shared point is the vertex and the two rays are the sides. Name an angle with three letters, always keeping the vertex in the middle, so $\angle ABC$ is the angle at $B$; the order of the outer two letters is free, so $\angle ABC=\angle CBA$, and when only one angle sits at a point you may just write $\angle B$. We measure how far an angle opens in degrees. A full turn all the way around is $360^\circ$, a straight line is half of that at $180^\circ$, and a square corner, a right angle, is $90^\circ$; a protractor reads the degrees straight off. Sort angles by how far they open: acute is below $90^\circ$, right is exactly $90^\circ$, obtuse is between $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$, straight is exactly $180^\circ$, and reflex is more than $180^\circ$, the big way around the outside.

In the diagram above, \(\angle ABC\) is a right angle (the small square at \(B\) marks it), and ray \(BX\) splits it into two parts. The diagram gives \(\angle XBC = 28^\circ\). Find \(\angle ABX\). Give the number of degrees.
hints: The little square tells you \(\angle ABC\) is a right angle, so the whole thing measures \(90^\circ\).
Ray \(BX\) cuts that right angle into \(\angle ABX\) and \(\angle XBC\), and those two parts add up to the full \(90^\circ\). You know one part already.
blocks.sol: The small square means \(\angle ABC\) is a right angle, so the two pieces ray \(BX\) makes must fill \(90^\circ\). One piece is given, so the other is what's left over. $$\angle ABX = 90^\circ - 28^\circ = \boxed{62}$$

blocks.x: In the diagram above, \(A\), \(O\), \(B\) lie on a straight line and ray \(OC\) stands on it. The diagram gives \(\angle AOC = 124^\circ\). Find \(\angle BOC\). Give the number of degrees.
hints: \(\angle AOC\) and \(\angle BOC\) sit side by side along the straight line \(AB\), so together they form a straight angle. How many degrees is a straight angle?
A straight angle is \(180^\circ\). Subtract the part you already know from \(180\) to get the part you want.
blocks.sol: Angles on a straight line add up to a straight angle, which is \(180^\circ\). Since \(\angle AOC\) and \(\angle BOC\) sit along line \(AB\), they add to \(180\). So $$\angle BOC = 180 - 124 = \boxed{56}.$$

blocks.x: In the diagram above, ray \(OB\) lies inside \(\angle AOC\), and \(\angle AOB = 88^\circ\). The whole angle \(\angle AOC = 147^\circ\). What is \(\angle BOC\)? Give the number of degrees.
hints: Ray \(OB\) cuts the big angle into two pieces, and those two pieces add up to the whole \(\angle AOC\).
You know the whole angle and one piece, so \(\angle BOC\) is what is left over. Subtract.
blocks.sol: The two parts \(\angle AOB\) and \(\angle BOC\) add up to the whole angle \(\angle AOC\). So \(\angle BOC\) is the whole minus the part you already know. $$\angle BOC = 147^\circ - 88^\circ = \boxed{59}$$

blocks.x: Adjacent angles add. When a ray splits a larger angle into two pieces sitting side by side, the pieces add up to the whole: $\angle AOB + \angle BOC = \angle AOC$. Read it forward to combine two angles into one, or backward to find a missing piece by subtracting the part you know from the whole. This one rule drives most angle chasing.
In the diagram above, \(\angle AOC\) is a right angle and ray \(OD\) splits it. Given that \(\angle AOD = 37^\circ\), find \(\angle DOC\). Give the number of degrees.
hints: A right angle measures \(90^\circ\), and ray \(OD\) breaks it into the two pieces \(\angle AOD\) and \(\angle DOC\).
Those two pieces together make the whole right angle, so \(\angle AOD + \angle DOC = 90^\circ\). Subtract to find the missing piece.
blocks.sol: Ray \(OD\) splits the right angle \(\angle AOC\) into two parts that add to \(90^\circ\). The two angles are complementary, so $$\angle DOC = 90 - 37 = \boxed{53}.$$

blocks.x: In the diagram above, two rays make an angle of \(110^\circ\), and the question mark marks the reflex angle, the big way around the outside. What is the reflex angle? Give the number of degrees.
hints: A full turn all the way around a point is \(360^\circ\). The reflex angle and the \(110^\circ\) angle together sweep out that whole turn.
So the reflex angle is what is left after you take the \(110^\circ\) away from \(360^\circ\).
blocks.sol:
The two rays split the full turn around the vertex into two pieces, the \(110^\circ\) angle and the reflex angle the long way around. Together they make one complete turn, which is \(360^\circ\). So subtract the small angle from the whole turn.
$$360^\circ - 110^\circ = \boxed{250}$$
The reflex angle is \(250\) degrees.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, ray \(OQ\) bisects \(\angle POR\), so the equal tick marks show the two halves are equal. Given that one half \(\angle POQ = 41^\circ\), what is the whole \(\angle POR\)? Give the number of degrees.
hints: A bisector cuts an angle into two equal halves, so \(\angle POR\) is made of two copies of \(\angle POQ\).
The whole is twice one half. Double \(41^\circ\).
blocks.sol: A bisector splits an angle into two equal halves, so the whole is twice one half. $$\angle POR = 2 \times 41^\circ = \boxed{82}^\circ$$

blocks.x: Special sums and bisectors. Two angles are complementary when they add to $90^\circ$, together filling a right angle, and supplementary when they add to $180^\circ$, filling a straight line. Whenever two angles sit side by side along a straight line, a linear pair, they are supplementary, so each is $180^\circ$ minus the other. A bisector is a ray from the vertex that cuts an angle into two equal halves; each half is half the whole, and the whole is twice either half. Equal tick marks on the two halves are the signal that a ray bisects.

In the diagram above, two straight lines cross at a point, forming four angles. One of them measures \(74^\circ\), and the angle marked with the question mark sits directly across from it. What is the marked angle? Give the number of degrees.
hints: The two marked angles are directly across the crossing point from each other. What is that pair of angles called?
Vertical angles, the pair sitting opposite each other where two lines cross, are always equal. So the marked angle matches the \(74^\circ\) one.
blocks.sol: When two straight lines cross, the angles directly opposite each other are called vertical angles, and vertical angles are always equal. The marked angle sits directly across from the \(74^\circ\) angle, so it has the same measure. $$\text{marked angle} = 74^\circ$$ The answer is \(\boxed{74}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, three rays leave a single point, cutting the whole way around into three angles. Two of them measure \(137^\circ\) and \(95^\circ\). Find the third angle, the one marked with the question mark. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Going all the way around a point is one full turn, and a full turn is \(360^\circ\). The three angles have to add up to that.
So the missing angle is what's left after you take the two known ones away from \(360^\circ\).
blocks.sol: The three angles wrap all the way around the point, so together they make a full turn of \(360^\circ\). Subtract the two you know to find the third. $$360 - 137 - 95 = \boxed{128}$$ The missing angle is \(128^\circ\).

blocks.x: Around a point. When two straight lines cross they make four angles; the two vertical angles directly across from each other are always equal, and each is supplementary to its neighbor, so one known angle unlocks all four. More generally, when angles meet around a single point with no gaps or overlaps, they sweep one full turn, so their measures add to $360^\circ$. Know all but one and you find the last by subtracting the rest from $360^\circ$.

Why does a full circle have exactly \(360^\circ\)? The choice traces back to the ancient Babylonians, who counted in base 60 rather than base 10. The number 360 sat happily in that system, and it lands close to the number of days in a year, so as the sun crept around the sky it moved almost one degree each day. Best of all, 360 divides evenly by a huge crowd of numbers, including 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, and 12. That made splitting a circle into equal parts easy, and thousands of years later we still measure angles their way.

In the diagram above, a ray splits the right angle \(\angle ABC\) into two parts whose measures are in the ratio \(1 : 5\). Find the smaller part, the one marked with the question mark. Give the number of degrees.
hints: A right angle is \(90^\circ\), and the two parts together make up that whole \(90^\circ\).
A ratio of \(1 : 5\) means the angle is cut into \(1 + 5 = 6\) equal shares. The smaller part is just one of those shares.
blocks.sol: The ratio \(1 : 5\) splits the right angle into \(1 + 5 = 6\) equal shares. Since a right angle is \(90^\circ\), each share is $$\frac{90^\circ}{6} = 15^\circ.$$ The smaller part is one share, so it measures \(\boxed{15}\) degrees.

blocks.x: The clock in the diagram above shows 5:00. Find the measure of the (non-reflex) angle between the hour hand and the minute hand. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The 12 hour marks split the full \(360^\circ\) evenly. How many degrees is one gap between neighboring marks?
Count how many of those gaps sit between the two hands at 5:00, then add up that many equal pieces.
blocks.sol: The 12 marks divide the full circle evenly, so each gap is \(\frac{360^\circ}{12} = 30^\circ\). At 5:00 the minute hand points at 12 and the hour hand points at 5, which is 5 gaps away. So the angle is \(5 \times 30^\circ = \boxed{150}\) degrees.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, a ray stands on a straight line, and the two angles it forms are labeled \(x^\circ\) and \((x+10)^\circ\). Find \(x\). Give the number of degrees (the value of \(x\)).
hints: The ray sits on a straight line, so the two angles together open up all the way across it. What does a straight angle measure?
The two labeled angles are supplementary, so they add to \(180^\circ\). Set up \(x + (x+10) = 180\) and solve for \(x\).
blocks.sol: A ray standing on a straight line makes two supplementary angles, so they add to \(180^\circ\). Adding the labels gives $$x + (x+10) = 180,$$ which simplifies to \(2x + 10 = 180\). Then \(2x = 170\), so \(x = \boxed{85}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, two rays split the straight angle into three angles whose measures are in the ratio \(2 : 3 : 4\). Find the largest of the three, the one marked with the question mark. Give the number of degrees.
hints: A straight angle measures \(180^\circ\), and the three parts together fill it. Think of the ratio \(2 : 3 : 4\) as splitting that \(180^\circ\) into equal shares.
Add the ratio numbers: \(2 + 3 + 4 = 9\). So \(180^\circ\) is cut into \(9\) equal shares. The largest angle takes \(4\) of them.
blocks.sol:
The three angles sit along a straight line, so they add to \(180^\circ\). The ratio \(2 : 3 : 4\) cuts that into \(2 + 3 + 4 = 9\) equal shares, so each share is \(\frac{180^\circ}{9} = 20^\circ\). The largest angle is \(4\) shares.
$$\frac{4}{9} \times 180^\circ = 4 \times 20^\circ = \boxed{80}$$

blocks.x: In the diagram above, an angle \(x^\circ\) and its supplement \((180-x)^\circ\) sit side by side on a straight line. The angle is \(15^\circ\) more than twice its supplement. Find the angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Two angles that sit together on a straight line are supplementary, so they add to \(180^\circ\). That is why the supplement is written \((180-x)^\circ\).
Turn the words into an equation. "\(15^\circ\) more than twice its supplement" means \(x = 2(180-x) + 15\). Expand the right side and collect the \(x\) terms.
blocks.sol:
The two angles lie together on a straight line, so they are supplements and the second one is \((180-x)^\circ\). The sentence says the angle equals twice its supplement plus \(15^\circ\), so
$$x = 2(180 - x) + 15.$$
Expanding the right side gives \(x = 360 - 2x + 15\). Collect the \(x\) terms to get \(3x = 375\), so \(x = 125\). The angle is \(\boxed{125}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, \(\angle ABC\) is a right angle and ray \(BX\) splits it into two smaller angles. Given that \(\angle XBC = 34^\circ\), what is \(\angle ABX\)? Give the number of degrees.
hints: A right angle measures \(90^\circ\), and ray \(BX\) breaks it into \(\angle ABX\) and \(\angle XBC\). Those two pieces together must make the whole right angle, so what must they add up to?
review.sol: The two pieces sit side by side and fill the right angle, so they add to \(90^\circ\). That means \(\angle ABX\) is whatever is left after taking away \(\angle XBC\). $$\angle ABX = 90^\circ - 34^\circ = \boxed{56}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, ray \(OB\) lies inside \(\angle AOC\), splitting it into two smaller angles. The diagram shows \(\angle AOB = 92^\circ\), and the whole \(\angle AOC = 155^\circ\). What is \(\angle BOC\)? Give the number of degrees.
hints: Ray \(OB\) breaks the big angle into two pieces that add up to the whole. So \(\angle AOB + \angle BOC = \angle AOC\). You know the whole and one piece, so subtract to find the other.
review.sol: The two smaller angles sit side by side and together fill the whole angle, so they add to it. $$\angle AOB + \angle BOC = \angle AOC$$ Put in what you know and solve for the missing piece. $$92^\circ + \angle BOC = 155^\circ$$ $$\angle BOC = 155^\circ - 92^\circ = \boxed{63}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, \(AOB\) is a straight line and ray \(OC\) rises from point \(O\). Given that \(\angle AOC = 113^\circ\), what is \(\angle BOC\)? Give the number of degrees.
hints: The angles \(\angle AOC\) and \(\angle BOC\) sit side by side on the straight line \(AOB\), so together they make a straight angle. A straight angle is \(180^\circ\), so what is left after taking away \(113^\circ\)?
review.sol:
Angles on a straight line add up to \(180^\circ\). Since \(\angle AOC\) and \(\angle BOC\) together form the straight line \(AOB\),
$$\angle BOC = 180^\circ - 113^\circ = \boxed{67}^\circ.$$

review.x: In the diagram above, \(\angle AOC\) is a right angle and ray \(OD\) falls inside it. Given that \(\angle AOD = 29^\circ\), what is \(\angle DOC\)? Give the number of degrees.
hints: A right angle measures \(90^\circ\), and \(\angle AOD\) and \(\angle DOC\) are the two pieces that fill it. What is left once you take away \(29^\circ\)?
review.sol: The two smaller angles sit inside the right angle and add up to it, so \(\angle AOD + \angle DOC = 90^\circ\). Subtract the part you know. $$\angle DOC = 90^\circ - 29^\circ = \boxed{61}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, ray \(OQ\) bisects \(\angle POR\), so the two halves are equal. Given that \(\angle POQ = 38^\circ\), what is the whole \(\angle POR\)? Give the number of degrees.
hints: A bisector cuts an angle into two equal parts, so \(\angle POR\) is made of two copies of \(\angle POQ\). What do you get when you put both halves back together?
review.sol: Since ray \(OQ\) bisects \(\angle POR\), the whole angle is two equal halves and each half is \(38^\circ\). Double one half. $$\angle POR = 2 \times 38^\circ = 76^\circ.$$ So \(\angle POR = \boxed{76}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, two lines cross and one of the four angles measures \(116^\circ\). The marked angle sits right beside it along the same straight line. What is the measure of the marked angle? Give the number of degrees.
hints: The marked angle and the \(116^\circ\) angle lie side by side along a straight line, so their measures add to \(180^\circ\). Subtract to find the leftover piece.
review.sol: The marked angle and the \(116^\circ\) angle are supplementary, since together they open up along a straight line. So their measures add to \(180^\circ\). $$180^\circ - 116^\circ = \boxed{64^\circ}$$ The marked angle is \(64\) degrees.

review.x: In the diagram above, three rays from a single point split the space all the way around into three angles. Two of them measure \(88^\circ\) and \(145^\circ\). What is the third angle? Give the number of degrees.
hints: The three angles wrap all the way around the point, so together they make a full turn of \(360^\circ\). Subtract the two you know from that.
review.sol:
The three angles fill a complete turn around the point, so they add up to \(360^\circ\). Subtract the two known angles from the full turn.
$$360 - 88 - 145 = \boxed{127}$$
The third angle is \(127^\circ\).

review.x: In the diagram above, two rays open up to make an angle of \(145^\circ\). The question mark marks the reflex angle, the one that sweeps all the way around the outside. What is the reflex angle? Give the number of degrees.
hints: A full turn around the vertex is \(360^\circ\). The marked \(145^\circ\) and the reflex angle together make that whole turn, so they add to \(360^\circ\).
review.sol: The two angles at the vertex fill one complete turn, so together they add to \(360^\circ\). Subtract the part you know from the whole turn. $$360^\circ - 145^\circ = \boxed{215}^\circ$$

review.x: In the diagram above, ray \(OP\) stands on a straight line, splitting the straight angle into \(x^\circ\) and \(2x^\circ\). Find \(x\). Give the number of degrees.
hints: A straight line makes a straight angle of \(180^\circ\), and the two pieces sitting on it add up to that. So \(x + 2x = 180\).
review.sol: The two angles rest on a straight line, so together they form a straight angle of \(180^\circ\). Adding the pieces gives $$x + 2x = 3x = 180,$$ so \(x = \boxed{60}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, ray \(BX\) splits the right angle \(\angle ABC\) into two parts whose measures are in the ratio \(2 : 7\). What is the measure of the smaller part? Give the number of degrees.
hints: A right angle is \(90^\circ\), and the two parts split it in the ratio \(2 : 7\). That means \(90^\circ\) is divided into \(2 + 7 = 9\) equal shares. The smaller part is \(2\) of those shares.
review.sol:
Split the right angle into equal shares. The ratio \(2 : 7\) means \(90^\circ\) is cut into \(2 + 7 = 9\) equal shares, so each share is \(90^\circ \div 9 = 10^\circ\). The smaller part is \(2\) shares.
$$\frac{2}{9} \times 90^\circ = 20^\circ$$
So the smaller part measures \(\boxed{20}\) degrees.

review.x: In the diagram above, the angle \(y^\circ\) and its complement \((90-y)^\circ\) together fill a right angle. The angle exceeds its complement by \(40^\circ\). What is \(y\)? Give the number of degrees.
hints: Complements add to \(90^\circ\), and "exceeds its complement by \(40\)" means \(y\) equals its complement plus \(40\). Write \(y = (90-y) + 40\) and solve for \(y\).
review.sol: The two angles are complementary, so they add to \(90^\circ\). Since \(y\) beats its complement by \(40\), write \(y = (90 - y) + 40\). The right side simplifies to \(130 - y\), so \(y = 130 - y\). Add \(y\) to both sides to get \(2y = 130\), and divide to get \(y = 65\). Check that the complement is \(25^\circ\) and \(65 - 25 = 40\), just as promised. So \(\boxed{65}\).

review.x: The clock in the diagram above reads 3:00. What is the non-reflex angle between the hour hand and the minute hand? Give the number of degrees.
hints: A full circle around the clock face is \(360^\circ\), and it is split evenly by the 12 hour marks. How many degrees is one hour-to-hour gap, and how many gaps sit between the two hands at 3:00?
review.sol: The clock face is a full turn of \(360^\circ\) divided into 12 equal hour steps, so each step is \(360^\circ \div 12 = 30^\circ\). At 3:00 the hour hand points at 3 and the minute hand points at 12, which are 3 steps apart. So the angle is \(3 \times 30^\circ = \boxed{90}^\circ\).

review.x: In the diagram above, three rays from a single point split the full turn around that point into three angles whose measures are in the ratio \(3 : 4 : 5\). What is the measure of the largest of these three angles? Give the number of degrees.
hints: The three angles wrap all the way around the point, so together they make a full turn of \(360^\circ\). The ratio \(3 : 4 : 5\) cuts that turn into \(3 + 4 + 5 = 12\) equal shares, and the largest angle takes \(5\) of them.
review.sol: The three angles go all the way around the point, so they add to a full turn of \(360^\circ\). The ratio \(3 : 4 : 5\) splits that turn into \(3 + 4 + 5 = 12\) equal shares, so one share is \(\frac{360^\circ}{12} = 30^\circ\). The largest angle is \(5\) shares. $$5 \times 30^\circ = \frac{5}{12} \times 360^\circ = \boxed{150}$$

blocks.x: Two lines in a plane that run side by side forever, always the same distance apart, never touching, are parallel. Now draw a third line straight across both of them. That crossing line is a transversal, and where it slices through, it opens up eight angles, four at each meeting point. You might expect eight different measurements. The surprise is that they are not all different. They lock together into a few small families, and the moment you know one angle, every other one falls into place.

Parallel lines and a transversal. Two lines in the same plane that never meet, staying exactly the same distance apart forever, are parallel, and matching arrowheads on the lines are the mark that says so. A line crossing both of them is a transversal, and at each crossing it makes four angles, eight in all. Because the two parallel lines run in the very same direction, those eight angles are not all different. They fall into a few small families of equal and supplementary angles, and the next ideas name those families one at a time.

In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines (the matching arrowheads mark them parallel). The \(65^\circ\) angle and the marked angle sit in the same position at their two crossings, so they are corresponding angles. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Corresponding angles are the pair that sit in the same spot at each crossing, like top-right at both. When the lines are parallel, that pair is always equal.
So the marked angle has the very same measure as the \(65^\circ\) angle. No adding or subtracting needed.
blocks.sol: The \(65^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are corresponding angles, matching positions at the two crossings. Across parallel lines, corresponding angles are equal, so the marked angle is \(\boxed{65}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, the \(72^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are alternate interior angles, sitting between the parallel lines on opposite sides of the transversal. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The two angles are alternate interior angles, and alternate interior angles formed by a transversal across parallel lines are always equal.
So the marked angle matches the \(72^\circ\) angle. Just read off that value.
blocks.sol: The two angles are alternate interior angles, so they are equal. That means the marked angle is the same as the given one. $$\angle = 72^\circ = \boxed{72}$$

blocks.x: In the diagram above, two parallel guide rails are crossed by a straight brace. The brace makes a \(62^\circ\) angle with the top rail, and the marked angle sits in the same position where the brace meets the bottom rail. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The brace is a transversal cutting the two parallel rails. The \(62^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are in matching positions, one at each rail, so they are corresponding angles.
Corresponding angles at parallel lines are equal, so the marked angle has the same measure as the given one.
blocks.sol: The two rails are parallel and the brace is the transversal. The \(62^\circ\) angle and the marked angle sit in the same position at each rail, so they are corresponding angles, which are equal. That gives $$\angle = 62^\circ = \boxed{62}$$

blocks.x: In the diagram above, the \(48^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are alternate exterior angles. They lie outside the parallel lines, on opposite sides of the transversal. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Alternate exterior angles sit outside the two parallel lines, on opposite sides of the transversal. When the lines are parallel, what is true about a pair of alternate exterior angles?
Alternate exterior angles are equal. So the marked angle matches the \(48^\circ\) angle exactly.
blocks.sol: The two marked angles are alternate exterior angles, and across parallel lines those are always equal. So the marked angle equals the given one. \(\boxed{48}\).

blocks.x: The equal families. Three pairs of angles come out equal whenever the lines are parallel. Corresponding angles sit in the same position at each crossing, like the upper right angle at both. Alternate interior angles lie between the two lines on opposite sides of the transversal, tracing a Z. Alternate exterior angles do the same thing but outside the two lines. All three pairs are equal, so once you spot which pair you have, you simply copy the angle across.

In the diagram above, the two marked angles sit side by side at the same crossing on the top line. One is \(65^\circ\) and the other is marked with a question mark. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The two angles sit next to each other on a straight line, so together they form a linear pair.
Angles in a linear pair are supplementary, meaning their measures add to \(180^\circ\).
blocks.sol: The \(65^\circ\) angle and the marked angle form a linear pair on a straight line, so they are supplementary and add to \(180^\circ\). $$180 - 65 = \boxed{115}$$ The marked angle is \(115^\circ\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, the \(110^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are co-interior angles, sitting between the parallel lines on the same side of the transversal. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Co-interior angles (the ones trapped between the parallel lines on the same side of the transversal) are supplementary, so the two angles add to \(180^\circ\).
Subtract the given angle from \(180^\circ\) to get the marked one.
blocks.sol: The \(110^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are co-interior, so they are supplementary and add to \(180^\circ\). That gives $$180 - 110 = \boxed{70}.$$ The marked angle is \(70^\circ\).

blocks.x: In the parallelogram \(ABCD\) above, \(\angle A = 73^\circ\). Find \(\angle C\), the angle opposite it. Give the number of degrees.
hints: \(\angle A\) and \(\angle C\) sit across from each other, so they are opposite angles of the parallelogram.
Opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal, so \(\angle C\) matches \(\angle A\).
blocks.sol: \(\angle A\) and \(\angle C\) are opposite angles of the parallelogram, and opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal. So $$\angle C = \angle A = \boxed{73}$$

blocks.x: Angles that add to 180, and the parallelogram. Not every pair is equal. Two angles side by side on a straight line form a linear pair and add to \(180^\circ\), and co-interior angles, the pair between the lines on the same side of the transversal, are supplementary too, adding to \(180^\circ\). So subtract from 180 rather than copy. A parallelogram folds this whole picture shut, since each pair of opposite sides is parallel. Its opposite angles are equal, and any two consecutive angles are co-interior, so they add to \(180^\circ\). One known angle hands you all four.

Now put these pairs to work as equations. When the two angles are given as expressions in \(x\), the same two moves are all you need. Set the expressions equal for corresponding or alternate angles, since those pairs are equal. Set their sum to \(180\) for co-interior angles, since those add to a straight angle. Either way you get one equation, and solving it for \(x\) unlocks every angle in the figure.

In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The \(130^\circ\) angle is at the top crossing and the marked angle is at the bottom crossing. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The \(130^\circ\) angle sits in the same position at the top crossing as one angle at the bottom crossing. Corresponding angles are equal, so that bottom angle is also \(130^\circ\).
That \(130^\circ\) angle and the marked angle sit on a straight line together, so they form a linear pair and add to \(180^\circ\).
blocks.sol: The \(130^\circ\) angle and the angle in the matching spot at the bottom crossing are corresponding angles, so that angle is also \(130^\circ\). The marked angle forms a linear pair with it, so the two add to \(180^\circ\). $$180 - 130 = \boxed{50}$$

blocks.x: In the diagram above, a transversal crosses two parallel lines and meets the top line at a right angle, shown by the small square. Find the marked angle where the transversal crosses the bottom line. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The transversal is perpendicular to the top line. Think about what that forces at the bottom line, since the two lines are parallel.
A line perpendicular to one of two parallel lines is perpendicular to the other one as well, so the marked angle is a right angle.
blocks.sol: The transversal meets the top line at a right angle, so it is perpendicular to that line. A line perpendicular to one of two parallel lines is perpendicular to the other one too. So the transversal meets the bottom line at a right angle as well, and the marked angle is \(\boxed{90}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines, and the two marked angles are corresponding angles labeled \(2x^\circ\) and \((x+40)^\circ\). Find \(x\). Give the number of degrees (the value of \(x\)).
hints: The two marked angles are corresponding angles, so they have equal measure. Set the two expressions equal to each other.
You get \(2x = x + 40\). Subtract \(x\) from both sides to get \(x\) by itself.
blocks.sol: The two marked angles are corresponding angles, so they are equal. That gives $$2x = x + 40.$$ Subtracting \(x\) from both sides leaves \(x = 40\), so \(\boxed{40}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The two co-interior angles are labeled \(3x^\circ\) and \(2x^\circ\). Find \(x\). Give the number of degrees (the value of \(x\)).
hints: The two marked angles are co-interior (same-side interior), so they are supplementary and add to \(180^\circ\). Set \(3x + 2x = 180\).
Combine the like terms into \(5x = 180\), then divide both sides by 5.
blocks.sol: The two marked angles are co-interior, so they are supplementary and add to \(180^\circ\). That gives $$3x + 2x = 180$$ so \(5x = 180\), and dividing by 5 gives \(x = \boxed{36}\).

blocks.x: Running the rules backward. Every rule here also works in reverse. So far you started with parallel lines and found equal or supplementary angles. Flip it: if a transversal makes corresponding angles equal, or alternate angles equal, or co-interior angles that add to \(180^\circ\), then the two lines it crosses HAVE to be parallel. This reversed version is the converse, and it is how you prove lines are parallel in the first place. When a problem hands you parallel lines, use the rules going forward. When it asks you to make the lines parallel, set the matching angles equal (or the co-interior ones summing to \(180^\circ\)) and solve.

In the diagram above, a transversal crosses two lines. The angles \(3x^\circ\) and \(75^\circ\) sit in matching positions, so they are corresponding angles. For the two lines to be parallel, these corresponding angles must be equal. Find \(x\). Give the number of degrees (the value of \(x\)).
hints: Corresponding angles are only equal when the lines are parallel. So for these lines to be parallel, \(3x^\circ\) has to equal \(75^\circ\).
Set the two expressions equal and solve. What times 3 gives 75?
blocks.sol: The two marked angles are corresponding angles, and parallel lines force corresponding angles to be equal. So $$3x = 75.$$ Divide both sides by 3 to get \(x = \boxed{25}\).

blocks.x: The idea that parallel lines never meet is one of five postulates Euclid wrote down in Greece more than two thousand years ago. Four of them felt obvious, but this one always felt like something you should be able to prove from the rest. For centuries mathematicians tried, and every one of them failed. Eventually a few of them tried the opposite, letting parallel lines behave differently, and instead of nonsense they discovered entirely new curved geometries. Those strange geometries are exactly the ones Einstein reached for when he described how gravity bends space.

In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. One angle at the top crossing measures \(116^\circ\), and the acute angle marked at the bottom crossing is unknown. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The \(116^\circ\) angle at the top corresponds to the angle in the same position at the bottom crossing, so that angle is also \(116^\circ\).
That \(116^\circ\) angle and the marked acute angle sit on a straight line together, so they form a linear pair and add to \(180^\circ\).
blocks.sol: This takes two steps. First use corresponding angles. The \(116^\circ\) angle at the top crossing matches the angle in the same position at the bottom crossing, so that bottom angle is also \(116^\circ\). That angle and the marked angle form a linear pair along the straight line, so they add to \(180^\circ\). $$180 - 116 = \boxed{64}$$ The marked angle is \(64^\circ\).

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The \(58^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are corresponding angles. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Corresponding angles sit in the same position at each intersection, so they are equal. What must the marked angle be?
review.sol: The \(58^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are corresponding angles, and corresponding angles are equal. So the marked angle is \(\boxed{58}\) degrees.

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The \(51^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are alternate interior angles. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Alternate interior angles sit on opposite sides of the transversal, between the two parallel lines. When the lines are parallel, they are equal.
review.sol: The marked angle and the \(51^\circ\) angle are alternate interior angles, and alternate interior angles are equal when the lines are parallel. So the marked angle is \(\boxed{51}\) degrees.

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The \(112^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are co-interior angles (same-side interior). Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Co-interior angles sit on the same side of the transversal between the parallel lines, and they are supplementary, so the two measures add to \(180^\circ\).
review.sol: Co-interior angles are supplementary, so they add to \(180^\circ\). $$180 - 112 = \boxed{68}$$ The marked angle is \(68^\circ\).

review.x: In the diagram above, the \(43^\circ\) angle and the marked angle sit side by side at the same crossing and form a linear pair. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The two angles form a linear pair, so together they make a straight angle. That means they are supplementary and add to \(180^\circ\).
review.sol: The two angles form a linear pair, so they are supplementary and add to \(180^\circ\). Subtract the known angle from \(180^\circ\). $$180 - 43 = \boxed{137}$$ The marked angle is \(137^\circ\).

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The \(39^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are alternate exterior angles. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Alternate exterior angles sit on opposite sides of the transversal, both outside the parallel lines. When the lines are parallel, they are equal.
review.sol: Alternate exterior angles are equal when the lines are parallel, so the marked angle matches the given one. $$\angle = 39^\circ = \boxed{39}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The \(125^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are corresponding angles. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Corresponding angles sit in the same position at each crossing, so they are equal.
review.sol: Corresponding angles are equal, so the marked angle matches the given one. $$\angle = 125^\circ = \boxed{125}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The two marked angles, \(2x^\circ\) and \((x+30)^\circ\), are corresponding angles. Find the value of \(x\). Give the value of \(x\).
hints: Corresponding angles are equal, so set the two expressions equal to each other and solve for \(x\).
review.sol: Corresponding angles are equal, so the two expressions match. $$2x = x + 30$$ Subtract \(x\) from both sides. $$x = \boxed{30}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines, and the two marked angles are \(5x^\circ\) and \(4x^\circ\). They are co-interior angles, so they sit on the same side of the transversal between the two parallel lines. Find \(x\). Give the value of \(x\).
hints: Co-interior angles (same-side interior angles) add up to \(180^\circ\). Set \(5x + 4x\) equal to \(180\).
review.sol: The two angles are co-interior, so they are supplementary and add to \(180^\circ\). That gives $$5x + 4x = 180$$ $$9x = 180$$ so \(\boxed{x = 20}\).

review.x: In the parallelogram above, \(\angle A = 108^\circ\). The angle you want, \(\angle B\), is consecutive to \(\angle A\). Find \(\angle B\). Give the number of degrees.
hints: Consecutive angles of a parallelogram are supplementary, so \(\angle A\) and \(\angle B\) add to \(180^\circ\).
review.sol: Consecutive angles of a parallelogram are supplementary, so \(\angle A\) and \(\angle B\) add to \(180^\circ\). That gives $$\angle B = 180 - 108 = \boxed{72}.$$ So \(\angle B = 72^\circ\).

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two lines, and the \(4x^\circ\) angle and the \(100^\circ\) angle are corresponding angles. For the two lines to be parallel, what must \(x\) be? Give the value of \(x\).
hints: Corresponding angles are equal only when the lines are parallel. So set the two expressions equal and solve for \(x\).
review.sol: The lines are parallel exactly when the corresponding angles are equal, so \(4x^\circ\) must equal \(100^\circ\). Set them equal and solve. $$4x = 100 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x = \boxed{25}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, the transversal meets the top parallel line at a right angle. Since the transversal is perpendicular to one of the parallel lines, it is perpendicular to the other as well. Find the marked angle where the transversal crosses the bottom line. Give the number of degrees.
hints: A transversal that is perpendicular to one of two parallel lines must be perpendicular to the other, so the marked angle is a right angle too.
review.sol: The transversal hits the top line at \(90^\circ\). Because the two lines are parallel, a transversal perpendicular to one is perpendicular to the other, so it meets the bottom line at a right angle as well. $$\angle = \boxed{90}^\circ$$

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The \(53^\circ\) angle and the marked angle are corresponding angles. Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Corresponding angles sit in the same position at each crossing, so they are equal.
review.sol: Corresponding angles are equal, so the marked angle matches the \(53^\circ\) angle. \(\boxed{53}\)

review.x: In the diagram above, a transversal cuts two parallel lines. The \(114^\circ\) angle at the top crossing and the marked angle at the bottom crossing are on the same side, and the marked angle forms a linear pair with the angle that corresponds to the \(114^\circ\). Find the marked angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The \(114^\circ\) angle corresponds to an equal \(114^\circ\) angle at the bottom crossing. That angle and the marked one sit together on a straight line, so they are supplementary.
review.sol: Corresponding angles are equal, so the top \(114^\circ\) matches a \(114^\circ\) angle at the bottom crossing. The marked angle forms a linear pair with it, so the two are supplementary. $$180 - 114 = \boxed{66}$$ The marked angle is \(66^\circ\).

blocks.x: You can measure a single angle now, and you know how angles line up when parallel lines are involved. Time to look inward. Every closed shape has angles tucked inside its corners, and those angles are not free to be anything they want. They have to add up. Start with the triangle, the simplest shape you can close with straight sides. Its three angles always land on the same total, no matter how you stretch or tilt it. Once you see why that happens, the same idea will carry you all the way up to a shape with any number of sides.
In the diagram above, a triangle has two angles marked \(47^\circ\) and \(72^\circ\). Find the third angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The three angles of a triangle always add to \(180^\circ\). Add the two you know, then see how much is left.
Subtract both known angles from \(180^\circ\): start with \(180 - 47\), then take away the \(72\).
blocks.sol: The three interior angles of a triangle add to \(180^\circ\), so the third is what is left after removing the other two. $$180 - 47 - 72 = \boxed{61}$$ The third angle is \(61^\circ\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, an isosceles triangle has its apex angle marked \(40^\circ\). The tick marks show the two sides meeting at the apex are equal, so the two base angles are equal. Find one base angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The three angles of any triangle add to \(180^\circ\). Subtract the apex angle to find how much the two base angles share between them.
The tick marks tell you the base angles are equal, so split the leftover evenly between the two of them.
blocks.sol: The three angles add to \(180^\circ\), so the two base angles together take up \(180-40 = 140\) degrees. The tick marks make those base angles equal, so each one is half of that. $$\frac{180^\circ - 40^\circ}{2} = \boxed{70^\circ}$$ One base angle is \(70\) degrees.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, one side of a triangle is extended, forming an exterior angle of \(110^\circ\). One of the two remote interior angles is \(65^\circ\). Find the other remote interior angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The exterior angle theorem says an exterior angle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles, the two corners that do not touch it.
Set \(65^\circ\) plus the unknown angle equal to \(110^\circ\), then solve for the unknown.
blocks.sol: By the exterior angle theorem, the exterior angle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles. So the unknown angle is $$110^\circ - 65^\circ = \boxed{45}^\circ.$$ The other remote interior angle is \(45^\circ\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, the three angles of a triangle are marked \(x^\circ\), \(2x^\circ\), and \(3x^\circ\). Find the largest angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The three angles of a triangle add to \(180^\circ\). Add the three marked pieces and set the total equal to \(180\).
Once you solve for \(x\), the largest angle is the one marked \(3x^\circ\), so multiply your \(x\) by \(3\).
blocks.sol: The three angles of a triangle add to \(180^\circ\), so the marked pieces satisfy $$x + 2x + 3x = 180.$$ That gives \(6x = 180\), so \(x = 30\). The largest angle is the one marked \(3x^\circ\), which is \(3 \times 30 = \boxed{90}\).

blocks.x: The angles of a triangle. The three interior angles of any triangle add to $180^\circ$ — slide the two base angles up to the apex along a parallel line and all three come to rest on one straight line. Push a side past a corner and the exterior angle that opens up equals the sum of the two remote interior angles it does not touch, since it shares a straight line with the third angle. And because the three angles must share a single $180^\circ$, a triangle can hold at most one right or obtuse angle: it is acute when all three are below $90^\circ$, right when one is exactly $90^\circ$, and obtuse when one is above.
In the diagram above is a hexagon. Find the sum of its interior angles. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The interior angles of any polygon add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\). Count the sides of the hexagon above to find \(n\).
A hexagon has 6 sides, so \(n=6\). Work out \((6-2)\times 180^\circ\).
blocks.sol: The interior angles of a polygon add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\). A hexagon has 6 sides, so \(n=6\). $$(6-2)\times 180^\circ = 4\times 180^\circ = \boxed{720^\circ}$$ The sum of the interior angles is 720 degrees.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, a pentagon has four interior angles marked \(100^\circ\), \(110^\circ\), \(120^\circ\), and \(130^\circ\). Find the fifth angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: A pentagon has 5 sides, so its interior angles add to \((5-2)\times 180^\circ\). Work out that total first.
Subtract the four marked angles from that total to find the missing fifth angle.
blocks.sol: The interior angles of an \(n\)-gon add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\). For a pentagon that is $$(5-2)\times 180^\circ = 540^\circ.$$ Subtract the four marked angles from the total. $$540^\circ - 100^\circ - 110^\circ - 120^\circ - 130^\circ = \boxed{80}^\circ$$ So the fifth angle is \(80^\circ\).

blocks.x: The interior angles of a polygon add to \(1080^\circ\). How many sides does it have? Give the number of sides.
hints: The interior angles of an \(n\)-sided polygon add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\). Set that equal to \(1080^\circ\).
Divide both sides by \(180\) to find \(n-2\), then add \(2\) to get \(n\).
blocks.sol: The interior angles of an \(n\)-sided polygon add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\), so set that total equal to \(1080^\circ\). $$(n-2)\times 180 = 1080$$ Divide both sides by \(180\) to get \(n-2 = 6\), then add \(2\) to both sides. The polygon has \(\boxed{8}\) sides.

blocks.x: Polygons and their angles. A polygon is a closed figure of straight sides; its corners are vertices, the angle inside each corner is an interior angle, and a diagonal joins two non-neighbouring vertices. We name them by side count: triangle 3, quadrilateral 4, pentagon 5, hexagon 6, octagon 8, decagon 10. Draw every diagonal from one vertex and an $n$-gon splits into $n-2$ triangles, each worth $180^\circ$, so its interior angles add to $$(n-2)\times 180^\circ.$$ A quadrilateral gives $360^\circ$, a pentagon $540^\circ$, and the total climbs $180^\circ$ for every side you add.
The diagram above shows a regular octagon with one interior angle marked. Find that angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: An octagon has \(8\) sides, so use the interior-angle formula for a regular polygon, \(\frac{(n-2)\times 180^\circ}{n}\), with \(n=8\).
First find \((8-2)\times 180^\circ\), the total of all eight angles, then split it evenly among the \(8\) corners.
blocks.sol: A regular polygon has equal angles, so each interior angle is \(\frac{(n-2)\times 180^\circ}{n}\). With \(n=8\), the eight angles together add to \((8-2)\times 180^\circ = 1080^\circ\). Sharing that evenly among the \(8\) corners gives $$\frac{1080^\circ}{8} = \boxed{135}^\circ.$$

blocks.x: The diagram above shows a regular 20-sided polygon with one interior angle marked. Find that angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: A regular polygon has all its interior angles equal, so each one is the total interior angle sum shared out evenly. That total is \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\), and here \(n=20\). Work out the total first.
Once you have the sum \((20-2)\times 180^\circ = 3240^\circ\) for all twenty corners, one corner is just an equal slice of it. Divide by how many corners there are.
blocks.sol: For any regular polygon, every interior angle equals \(\frac{(n-2)\times 180^\circ}{n}\), since the whole interior sum \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\) is split evenly among the \(n\) equal corners. Here \(n=20\), so the twenty corners together hold \((20-2)\times 180^\circ = 18\times 180^\circ = 3240^\circ\). One corner is that shared out over all twenty. $$\frac{(20-2)\times 180^\circ}{20} = \frac{3240^\circ}{20} = \boxed{162}^\circ$$ Quick check, twenty angles of \(162^\circ\) rebuild the full \(3240^\circ\), so the marked angle is \(162\) degrees.

blocks.x: Regular polygons. A regular polygon has all sides equal and all angles equal, so each interior angle is simply the whole interior sum shared evenly among the corners: $$\frac{(n-2)\times 180^\circ}{n}.$$ A regular pentagon gives $540/5 = 108^\circ$ and a regular hexagon $720/6 = 120^\circ$.
In the diagram above, a regular pentagon has one exterior angle marked. Find that exterior angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The exterior angles of any polygon always add to \(360^\circ\), no matter how many sides.
A regular pentagon has 5 equal corners, so split that total evenly.
blocks.sol: The exterior angle sum is \(360^\circ\) for every polygon. A regular pentagon has 5 equal exterior angles, so divide the total by 5. $$\frac{360^\circ}{5} = 72^\circ$$ The marked exterior angle is \(\boxed{72}\) degrees.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, one corner of a regular polygon is shown, and each interior angle is \(150^\circ\). How many sides does the polygon have? Give the number of sides.
hints: At each corner the interior angle and the exterior angle sit on a straight line, so they add to \(180^\circ\). Find the exterior angle first.
Walking once around a polygon, the exterior angles add up to \(360^\circ\). Divide that total by one exterior angle to count the corners.
blocks.sol: The interior and exterior angle at a corner form a straight line, so the exterior angle is \(180^\circ - 150^\circ = 30^\circ\). The exterior angles of any polygon add to \(360^\circ\), and here they are all equal, so$$n = \frac{360^\circ}{30^\circ} = \boxed{12}.$$

blocks.x: The exterior angles close a full turn. Walk once around any convex polygon, turning at each corner by its exterior angle, and you complete exactly one loop — so the exterior angles always add to $360^\circ$, whatever the side count. On a regular $n$-gon they are equal, so each is $\frac{360^\circ}{n}$, and each interior angle is its straight-line partner, $180^\circ$ minus the exterior angle. Read backwards, $n = \frac{360^\circ}{\text{exterior angle}}$ is the quickest way from an angle straight back to the number of sides.

Bees build their honeycombs out of regular hexagons, and there is a reason. Only three regular shapes tile a flat surface with no gaps, the triangle, the square, and the hexagon. Of those three, the hexagon walls off the most space for the least wall, so a honeycomb full of hexagons stores the most honey using the least wax. Bees found the best answer long before anyone could prove it. Mathematicians only finished proving the hexagon really is the best possible in 1999.
In the diagram above, a regular pentagon and a regular hexagon share a side and meet at a point, leaving a gap. Find the gap angle marked at that point. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The three angles around the shared point make a full turn, so they add to \(360^\circ\). Two of them are the corner angles of the polygons.
A regular pentagon has interior angle \(\frac{(5-2)\times 180^\circ}{5}\) and a regular hexagon \(\frac{(6-2)\times 180^\circ}{6}\). Subtract both from \(360^\circ\).
blocks.sol: The angles around the shared point make one full turn, so they **add to \(360^\circ\)**. The pentagon's corner is \(\frac{(5-2)\times 180^\circ}{5}=108^\circ\) and the hexagon's is \(\frac{(6-2)\times 180^\circ}{6}=120^\circ\). The gap is what's left. $$360^\circ - 108^\circ - 120^\circ = \boxed{132}^\circ$$

blocks.x: In the diagram above, one corner of a regular polygon is shown where each interior angle is \(4\) times its exterior angle. How many sides does the polygon have? Give the number of sides.
hints: At any corner the interior and exterior angle sit on a straight line, so they add to \(180^\circ\). Call the exterior angle \(e\) and write the interior angle as \(4e\).
Once you know one exterior angle, the number of sides comes from \(\frac{360^\circ}{n} = e\).
blocks.sol: The interior and exterior angle at a corner form a linear pair, so they add to \(180^\circ\). The interior angle is \(4\) times the exterior angle, so with exterior angle \(e\), $$4e + e = 180^\circ,\qquad 5e = 180^\circ,\qquad e = 36^\circ.$$ Every exterior angle of a regular polygon is \(\frac{360^\circ}{n}\), so \(\frac{360^\circ}{n} = 36^\circ\), which gives \(n = \boxed{10}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, a triangle has angles \(55^\circ\) and \(80^\circ\). Find the third angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The three angles of a triangle always add to \(180^\circ\). Subtract the two known angles from \(180^\circ\).
review.sol: The angles in any triangle add to \(180^\circ\). Two of them are \(55^\circ\) and \(80^\circ\), so the third is what is left after taking those away from \(180^\circ\). $$180^\circ - 55^\circ - 80^\circ = \boxed{45}$$ The third angle is \(45^\circ\).

review.x: In the diagram above, an isosceles triangle has an apex angle of \(100^\circ\), and the tick marks show that the two base angles are equal. Find one base angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The three angles of any triangle add to \(180^\circ\). Take away the apex angle, then split what is left evenly between the two equal base angles.
review.sol: The angles in a triangle add to \(180^\circ\), so the two base angles together make \(180^\circ - 100^\circ = 80^\circ\). The tick marks tell us those base angles are equal, so each one is half of that. $$\frac{80^\circ}{2} = \boxed{40}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, one side of a triangle is extended to make an exterior angle of \(125^\circ\). One of the two remote interior angles is \(60^\circ\). Find the other remote interior angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: An exterior angle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles, so the two remote angles have to add up to \(125^\circ\). You already know one of them.
review.sol: By the exterior angle theorem, an exterior angle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles. So the two remote angles add to \(125^\circ\). One of them is \(60^\circ\), so the other is $$125^\circ - 60^\circ = \boxed{65^\circ}.$$

review.x: In the diagram above, a triangle has angles \(2x^\circ\), \(3x^\circ\), and \(4x^\circ\). Find the largest angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The three angles of a triangle add to \(180^\circ\). Add the three expressions, set the sum equal to \(180\), and solve for \(x\) first.
review.sol: The angles in a triangle sum to \(180^\circ\), so add the three expressions and set them equal to \(180\). $$2x + 3x + 4x = 180$$ That gives \(9x = 180\), so \(x = 20\). The largest angle is \(4x\), which is \(4\times 20 = \boxed{80}\).

review.x: The diagram above shows a heptagon (a polygon with 7 sides). Find the sum of its interior angles. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Use the interior angle sum rule \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\). A heptagon has 7 sides, so substitute \(n=7\).
review.sol: By the interior angle sum rule, the angles of an \(n\)-sided polygon add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\). A heptagon has \(n=7\) sides, so $$(7-2)\times 180^\circ = 5\times 180^\circ = \boxed{900}^\circ.$$ The sum of the interior angles is \(900\) degrees.

review.x: In the diagram above, five of the six interior angles of a hexagon are \(110^\circ\), \(130^\circ\), \(115^\circ\), \(125^\circ\), and \(120^\circ\). Find the sixth angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The interior angles of a hexagon add to \((6-2)\times 180^\circ\). Add the five you know, then subtract from that total.
review.sol: A hexagon has \(n=6\) sides, so its interior angles add to \((6-2)\times 180^\circ = 720^\circ\). The five marked angles add to $$110^\circ + 130^\circ + 115^\circ + 125^\circ + 120^\circ = 600^\circ.$$ The sixth angle is what is left over, so \(720^\circ - 600^\circ = \boxed{120}\) degrees.

review.x: The interior angles of a polygon add to \(1440^\circ\). How many sides does it have? Give the number of sides.
hints: The interior angles of a polygon with \(n\) sides add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\). Set that equal to \(1440^\circ\) and solve for \(n\).
review.sol: By the polygon angle sum, the interior angles of an \(n\)-sided polygon add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\). Set this equal to the total. $$(n-2)\times 180^\circ = 1440^\circ$$ Divide both sides by \(180^\circ\) to get \(n-2 = 8\), so \(n = \boxed{10}\).

review.x: The diagram above shows a regular dodecagon, a polygon with \(12\) equal sides and \(12\) equal angles. Find the measure of one interior angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: The interior angles of any polygon add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\). Since this polygon is regular, every angle is equal, so divide that total by the number of angles.
review.sol: A regular polygon spreads its interior angle total evenly across all \(n\) corners, so each angle is \(\frac{(n-2)\times 180^\circ}{n}\). Here \(n = 12\). $$\frac{(12-2)\times 180^\circ}{12} = \frac{1800^\circ}{12} = \boxed{150^\circ}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, a regular polygon has 15 sides, with one interior angle marked. Find the size of that angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: Every angle in a regular polygon is equal, so use \(\frac{(n-2)\times 180^\circ}{n}\) with \(n=15\).
review.sol: The interior angles of any polygon add to \((n-2)\times 180^\circ\), and a regular polygon splits that total evenly across all \(n\) angles. With \(n=15\), $$\frac{(15-2)\times 180^\circ}{15} = \frac{2340^\circ}{15} = \boxed{156^\circ}.$$

review.x: In the diagram above, a regular decagon has one exterior angle marked. Find that exterior angle. Give the number of degrees.
hints: A regular polygon's exterior angles all share the full turn equally, so each one is \(\frac{360^\circ}{n}\). Count the sides to get \(n\).
review.sol: A decagon has \(n=10\) sides. In a regular polygon the exterior angles are all equal and together make one full turn, so each one is the exterior angle rule \(\frac{360^\circ}{n}\). $$\frac{360^\circ}{10} = \boxed{36}$$ Each exterior angle of the regular decagon is \(36^\circ\).

review.x: In the diagram above, one corner of a regular polygon has an exterior angle of \(45^\circ\), where a side is extended past the vertex. How many sides does the polygon have? Give the number of sides.
hints: Every exterior angle of a regular polygon is \(\frac{360^\circ}{n}\), since the exterior angles all add up to one full turn. Set that equal to the marked angle and solve for \(n\).
review.sol: Walk all the way around the polygon and you turn through one full circle, so the exterior angles add to \(360^\circ\). In a regular polygon they are all equal, which makes each one \(\frac{360^\circ}{n}\). The diagram marks that angle as \(45^\circ\), so $$\frac{360^\circ}{n}=45^\circ \;\Rightarrow\; n=\frac{360}{45}=\boxed{8}.$$ The polygon has \(8\) sides.

review.x: The diagram above shows one corner of a regular polygon, where each interior angle is \(140^\circ\). How many sides does the polygon have? Give the number of sides.
hints: The interior and exterior angles at a corner form a straight angle, so start by finding the exterior angle. Then remember that all the exterior angles of a polygon add to \(360^\circ\).
review.sol: Since the interior angle and the exterior angle sit on a straight line, the exterior angle is \(180^\circ - 140^\circ = 40^\circ\). For a regular polygon the exterior angle is \(\frac{360^\circ}{n}\), so $$n = \frac{360^\circ}{40^\circ} = \boxed{9}.$$ The polygon has 9 sides.

review.x: In the diagram above, one corner of a regular polygon is marked, and its interior angle is \(5\) times its exterior angle. How many sides does the polygon have? Give the number of sides.
hints: At any corner the interior and exterior angles are a linear pair, so they add to \(180^\circ\). Write the interior angle as \(5e\) and the exterior angle as \(e\), then find \(e\). Once you know the exterior angle, use \(\frac{360^\circ}{n}\).
review.sol: Interior and exterior angles at a corner form a linear pair, so they add to \(180^\circ\). The interior angle is \(5\) times the exterior angle, so with exterior angle \(e\), $$5e + e = 180^\circ,\quad 6e = 180^\circ,\quad e = 30^\circ.$$ For a regular polygon the exterior angle is \(\frac{360^\circ}{n}\), so $$\frac{360^\circ}{n} = 30^\circ \;\Rightarrow\; n = \frac{360^\circ}{30^\circ} = \boxed{12}.$$

blocks.x: You have spent three lessons on angles, the turn inside a shape. But a shape is made of two things, and the other one is its sides. A segment is just the straight path between two points, and its length is the distance across it. Over the next few lessons you will split a segment into pieces, add those pieces back together, and walk the whole way around a shape to measure how far you traveled.
In the diagram above, \(M\) is the midpoint of \(AB\) and the tick marks show the two halves are equal. Given \(AM = 7\), find \(AB\). Give the number of units.
hints: A midpoint splits a segment into two equal pieces, so \(MB\) has the same length as \(AM\).
The whole \(AB\) is those two equal halves added together. Double the \(7\).
blocks.sol: The midpoint \(M\) cuts \(AB\) into two equal halves, so \(MB = AM = 7\). The whole segment is both halves together, which means \(AB = 2 \times 7\). So \(AB = \boxed{14}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, \(M\) is the midpoint of \(AB\) with \(AB = 24\), and \(N\) is the midpoint of \(MB\). Find \(AN\). Give the number of units.
hints: Start with \(M\). A midpoint splits the whole into two equal halves, so what does that make \(AM\) and \(MB\)?
Now \(N\) sits in the middle of \(MB\), which gives you \(MN\). Then \(AN\) is \(AM\) and \(MN\) added together.
blocks.sol: Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(AB\), it cuts the whole in half, so \(AM = MB = 12\). Now \(N\) is the midpoint of \(MB\), which cuts that half in half again, so \(MN = 6\). Going from \(A\) to \(N\) covers \(AM\) then \(MN\), so $$AN = AM + MN = 12 + 6 = \boxed{18}.$$ So \(AN = 18\).

blocks.x: Segments, length, and the midpoint. A segment is the straight path between two endpoints, and we name it by them, like segment \(AB\). Its length is the distance across it, written just \(AB\) with the units dropped, so \(AB = 12\) means twelve units long. The midpoint is the point exactly halfway between the endpoints: it splits the segment into two equal halves. If \(M\) is the midpoint of \(AB\), then \(AM = MB = \frac{1}{2}AB\), and the whole is twice a half, \(AB = 2\,AM\). Matching tick marks on the two halves are the diagram telling you the point is a midpoint, so you can read \(AM = MB\) without measuring.
In the diagram above, \(B\) lies on \(AC\) with \(AB = 8\), and the bracket shows the whole segment \(AC = 20\). Find \(BC\). Give the number of units.
hints: The two pieces \(AB\) and \(BC\) together make up the whole segment \(AC\), so \(AB + BC = AC\).
Put in the lengths you know and subtract to solve for \(BC\).
blocks.sol: Point \(B\) splits \(AC\) into \(AB\) and \(BC\), and those two pieces add up to the whole, so \(AB + BC = AC\). Fill in what you know, \(8 + BC = 20\), and subtract to get $$BC = 20 - 8 = \boxed{12}.$$ So \(BC = 12\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, \(AB\), \(BC\), and \(CD\) each carry one tick mark, so the three pieces are equal, and \(AD = 27\). Find the length of \(AB\). Give the number of units.
hints: The tick marks tell you \(AB\), \(BC\), and \(CD\) are all the same length, and together they make up \(AD\).
Three equal pieces fill \(AD = 27\), so split that whole into three.
blocks.sol: The three tick marks mean \(AB\), \(BC\), and \(CD\) are equal, and stacked end to end they make the whole segment \(AD = 27\). So three equal pieces add to \(27\), and each one is $$27 \div 3 = \boxed{9}.$$ So \(AB = 9\).

blocks.x: Adding pieces, and reading tick marks. When a point \(B\) lies on segment \(AC\) between the ends, the two pieces add back to the whole. $$AB + BC = AC$$ Read it forward to combine two known lengths into the total, and backward to find a missing piece by subtracting from the whole: if \(AC = 20\) and \(AB = 8\), then \(BC = 20 - 8 = 12\). This is the segment version of angle addition. Tick marks tell you when segments are congruent — equal in length. Segments with the same number of ticks are equal, so several equal pieces in a row build a whole of one length times the number of pieces: five equal pieces of length \(3\) make \(5 \times 3 = 15\).
The diagram above shows a rectangle that is \(8\) units wide and \(5\) units tall. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the distance all the way around, so add up the lengths of all four sides.
A rectangle has two widths and two heights. You can add \(8 + 5 + 8 + 5\), or take \(2\times(8+5)\).
blocks.sol: The perimeter is the sum of all four side lengths. A rectangle has two sides of length \(8\) and two of length \(5\), so $$8 + 5 + 8 + 5 = 26.$$ A quicker way is to add one width and one height, then double it, \(2\times(8+5) = 26\). The perimeter is \(\boxed{26}\).

blocks.x: The diagram above shows a regular hexagon. All six sides are equal, marked by the ticks, and its perimeter is \(66\). Find the length of one side. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the sum of all the side lengths. Since the ticks tell you every side is equal, the six equal sides add up to \(66\).
Six equal sides summing to \(66\) means one side is \(66\) split into 6 equal parts. Divide to find it.
blocks.sol: A regular hexagon has 6 equal sides, so the perimeter is one side length added six times. To get a single side, split the perimeter into 6 equal parts. $$66 \div 6 = 11$$ So one side is \(\boxed{11}\).

blocks.x: The diagram above shows an L-shaped figure with its six side lengths marked \(6\), \(2\), \(4\), \(3\), \(2\), and \(5\). Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the distance all the way around, so it is the sum of every side. An L-shape has six sides, and each one is marked in the diagram.
Add all six lengths together in one running total, making sure you use each marked number exactly once.
blocks.sol: The perimeter is the sum of all the sides, so add the six marked lengths. $$6 + 2 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 5 = 22$$ The perimeter is \(\boxed{22}\).

blocks.x: The diagram above shows an isosceles triangle whose two equal legs, marked with ticks and labelled \(2x\), are each twice its base, labelled \(x\). The perimeter is \(40\). Find the base. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the sum of all three sides, so add the two legs and the base and set that equal to \(40\).
The two legs are each \(2x\) and the base is \(x\), so combine the like terms into one \(x\) term before solving.
blocks.sol: The perimeter is the sum of the three sides, so \(2x + 2x + x = 40\). Combining the like terms gives \(5x = 40\), so \(x = 8\). The base is \(x\), which is \(\boxed{8}\).

blocks.x: Perimeter. The perimeter of a closed figure is the total distance around it: add the lengths of all its sides. For a triangle with sides \(5\), \(6\), and \(7\), the perimeter is \(5 + 6 + 7 = 18\). Walk the outside, add each edge you cross, and the running total when you return to the start is the perimeter. For a regular polygon every side is equal, so you need not add one by one — just multiply one side by the number of sides. A regular hexagon with side \(4\) has perimeter \(6 \times 4 = 24\).

In the diagram above, two sides of a triangle are \(7\) and \(9\), and the third side (marked with a question mark) is a whole number. Find the largest the third side could be. Give the number of units.
hints: The third side of a triangle is always shorter than the other two sides added together. Add the two given sides.
Once you have that sum, the third side has to be less than it. Since the side is a whole number, take the biggest whole number below that sum.
blocks.sol: Any side of a triangle must be less than the sum of the other two. The two given sides add to \(7 + 9 = 16\), so the third side must satisfy the third side \(< 16\). The largest whole number under \(16\) is \(15\). If the third side were \(16\), the triangle would flatten into a straight line, so it truly has to stay below \(16\). The answer is \(\boxed{15}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, two sides of a triangle are \(7\) and \(9\), and the third side (marked with a question mark) is a whole number. What is the smallest the third side could be? Give the number of units.
hints: The triangle inequality says any two sides must sum to more than the third. Turn that around: the third side must be more than the difference of the other two.
Find \(9 - 7\), then pick the smallest whole number that is strictly larger than it.
blocks.sol: By the triangle inequality, the third side has to be more than the difference of the other two, or the triangle collapses flat. That difference is \(9 - 7 = 2\), so the third side must be more than \(2\). The smallest whole number bigger than \(2\) is \(\boxed{3}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, two sides of a triangle are \(4\) and \(9\), and the third side (the question mark) is a whole number. How many different whole-number lengths are possible for it? Give the number of whole-number lengths.
hints: The triangle inequality says the third side has to be less than the sum of the other two and more than their difference. Work out both of those bounds from \(4\) and \(9\).
The third side must be more than \(9 - 4\) and less than \(9 + 4\). Count the whole numbers strictly between those two bounds.
blocks.sol: The triangle inequality traps the third side between the sum and the difference of the other two sides. It has to be less than \(9 + 4 = 13\) and more than \(9 - 4 = 5\), so $$5 < \text{third side} < 13.$$ The whole numbers strictly between \(5\) and \(13\) are \(6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12\). That is \(\boxed{7}\) possible lengths.

blocks.x: The triangle inequality. Three lengths close into a triangle only when the two shorter ones together are longer than the longest. The two short sides must stretch across the long one and still meet at a corner; if they fall short, no triangle forms. Put simply, any two sides of a triangle add to more than the third. A useful consequence: the unknown third side must be less than the sum and more than the difference of the other two, which pins down exactly which lengths are allowed.

The word perimeter comes straight from Greek. "Peri" means around and "metron" means measure, so perimeter literally says "measure around". That same "peri" turns up in a periscope, which lets you see around a corner, and in a perimeter fence that runs all the way around a property. Once you spot the "peri", you can hear the meaning hiding inside the word.
In the diagram above is a triangular garden with sides \(13\), \(14\), and \(15\) feet. Fencing costs \(\$4\) per foot. Find the total cost to fence it. Give the number of dollars.
hints: The fence runs all the way around, so start with the perimeter, the sum of the three sides.
Once you have the perimeter in feet, multiply by the cost of a single foot.
blocks.sol: Fencing wraps the whole border, so first find the perimeter by adding the three sides. \(13 + 14 + 15 = 42\) feet. Every foot costs \(\$4\), so multiply.$$42 \times 4 = \boxed{168}$$The garden costs \(168\) dollars to fence.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, points \(B\) and \(C\) lie on segment \(AD\) with \(AC\) and \(CD\) in the ratio \(3 : 1\), and \(B\) is the midpoint of \(AC\). The bracket shows \(BC = 6\). Find \(AD\). Give the number of units.
hints: Since \(B\) is the midpoint of \(AC\), the bracket \(BC = 6\) is exactly half of \(AC\). Use that to find the whole of \(AC\) first.
Once you know \(AC\), the ratio \(AC : CD = 3 : 1\) tells you \(CD\). Then \(AD\) is just \(AC + CD\).
blocks.sol: The midpoint \(B\) splits \(AC\) into two equal halves, so \(BC\) is half of \(AC\). That gives \(AC = 2 \times 6 = 12\). The ratio \(AC : CD = 3 : 1\) means \(CD\) is a third of \(AC\), so \(CD = 12 \div 3 = 4\). Then \(AD = AC + CD = 12 + 4 = \boxed{16}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, \(M\) is the midpoint of \(PQ\) and \(PM = 11\). Find \(PQ\). Give the number of units.
hints: A midpoint splits the segment into two equal halves, so \(PQ\) is made of two copies of \(PM\).
review.sol: Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(PQ\), the two halves \(PM\) and \(MQ\) are equal, so \(PQ\) is twice one half. $$PQ = 2 \times 11 = \boxed{22}$$ So \(PQ = 22\).

review.x: In the diagram above, \(B\) lies on \(AC\) with \(AB = 13\), and the bracket marks the whole length \(AC = 30\). Find \(BC\). Give the number of units.
hints: The whole \(AC\) is made of \(AB\) and \(BC\) together, so subtract the part you know from the whole.
review.sol: The point \(B\) splits \(AC\) into two pieces, \(AB\) and \(BC\), that add up to the whole. So \(BC = AC - AB\). Reading the diagram, \(AC = 30\) and \(AB = 13\), which gives $$BC = 30 - 13 = \boxed{17}.$$

review.x: In the diagram above, the tick marks show that \(XY\), \(YZ\), \(ZW\), and \(WV\) are all equal, and the whole length \(XV = 32\). Find \(XY\). Give the number of units.
hints: The four equal pieces together make up \(XV\). One piece is that whole length shared equally among the four.
review.sol: The four tick marks mean \(XY\), \(YZ\), \(ZW\), and \(WV\) are equal pieces that add up to \(XV\). So one piece is a quarter of the whole. $$XY = \frac{32}{4} = \boxed{8}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, \(M\) is the midpoint of \(AB\) with \(AB = 20\), and \(N\) is the midpoint of \(AM\). Find \(NB\). Give the number of units.
hints: Work outward from \(A\). Each midpoint splits a segment into two equal halves, so first find \(AM\), then \(AN\), and finally take \(NB = AB - AN\).
review.sol: Each midpoint cuts a segment in half. Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(AB\), we get \(AM = \frac{20}{2} = 10\). Since \(N\) is the midpoint of \(AM\), we get \(AN = \frac{10}{2} = 5\). Then \(N\) sits \(5\) units from \(A\), so $$NB = AB - AN = 20 - 5 = \boxed{15}.$$

review.x: The diagram above shows a rectangle that is \(9\) wide and \(6\) tall. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the distance all the way around, so add up every side. A rectangle has two widths and two heights, so you can add one width and one height and then double the result.
review.sol: The perimeter is the sum of all four sides. A rectangle has two sides of length \(9\) and two of length \(6\), so add one of each and double it. $$2\times(9+6) = 2\times 15 = 30$$ The perimeter is \(\boxed{30}\).

review.x: The diagram above shows an L-shaped figure with its six sides labelled. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the total distance around the shape, so add up all six side lengths.
review.sol: The perimeter is the sum of every side. Reading the six lengths off the figure and adding them, $$7 + 2 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 5 = \boxed{24}.$$ So the perimeter is \(24\).

review.x: In the diagram above, the two equal legs of the isosceles triangle each measure \(2x\), the base measures \(x\), and the perimeter is \(35\). Find the base. Give the number of units.
hints: Add the three sides in terms of \(x\) and set that sum equal to \(35\). The base you want is \(x\) itself, so solve for \(x\).
review.sol: The perimeter is the sum of the three sides, so add the two legs and the base. Each leg is \(2x\) and the base is \(x\), which gives $$2x + 2x + x = 35.$$ Combine the like terms to get \(5x = 35\), so \(x = 7\). The base is \(x\), so its length is \(\boxed{7}\).

review.x: The diagram above shows a regular octagon with perimeter \(72\). Since all eight sides are equal, find the length of one side. Give the number of units.
hints: A regular octagon has 8 equal sides, so divide the perimeter by the number of sides.
review.sol: All eight sides of a regular octagon are equal, so one side is the perimeter split into \(8\) equal parts. $$72 \div 8 = 9$$ One side has length \(\boxed{9}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, two sides of the triangle measure \(8\) and \(11\), and the third side is a whole number. What is the largest length the third side can be? Give the number of units.
hints: Any two sides of a triangle must add to more than the third side. So the third side has to be less than \(8 + 11\). What is the biggest whole number below that?
review.sol: By the triangle inequality, the two known sides must sum to more than the third side, so the third side is less than \(8 + 11 = 19\). The largest whole number below \(19\) is \(\boxed{18}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, a triangle has two sides of length \(8\) and \(11\), and its third side is a whole number of units. What is the smallest length that third side can be? Give the number of units.
hints: The triangle inequality says any two sides together must be longer than the third. So the third side has to be more than \(11 - 8\). What whole number just clears that?
review.sol: For a triangle to close up, the third side must be longer than the difference of the other two, so \(11 - 8 = 3\). The side has to be more than \(3\), and the smallest whole number that beats \(3\) is \(\boxed{4}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, two sides of a triangle are \(6\) and \(10\), and the third side is a whole number. How many different whole-number lengths could the third side be? Give the number of whole-number lengths.
hints: The triangle inequality says any two sides must add up to more than the third. Use that to bound the third side from below with \(10-6\) and from above with \(10+6\), then count the whole numbers strictly between them.
review.sol: By the triangle inequality, the third side must be less than the sum of the other two and more than their difference. So it is more than \(10-6=4\) and less than \(10+6=16\), giving \(4 < \text{third} < 16\). The whole numbers strictly between \(4\) and \(16\) run from \(5\) to \(15\), which is \(15-5+1=11\) values. The answer is \(\boxed{11}\).

review.x: The diagram above shows a square garden that measures \(14\) feet on each side. Fencing costs \(\$5\) per foot, and you want to fence the whole garden. How much will the fencing cost? Give the number of dollars.
hints: Fencing wraps the whole border, so first find the perimeter by adding up all four equal sides. Then every foot of that perimeter costs the same \(\$5\), so multiply the perimeter by \(5\).
review.sol: Fencing follows the border, so start with the perimeter. A square has four equal sides, so the perimeter is \(4 \times 14 = 56\) feet. Each foot costs \(\$5\), so the fencing costs $$56 \times 5 = \boxed{280}$$ dollars.

review.x: In the diagram above, \(B\) and \(C\) lie on segment \(AD\) with \(AB : BC : CD = 3 : 4 : 5\), and \(AD = 36\). Find \(BC\). Give the number of units.
hints: The three parts add up to \(3 + 4 + 5\) equal pieces that fill all of \(AD\). Find the size of one piece first, then take the number of pieces \(BC\) is worth.
review.sol: The ratio splits \(AD\) into \(3 + 4 + 5 = 12\) equal pieces. So one piece is $$36 \div 12 = 3.$$ Now \(BC\) is worth \(4\) of those pieces, so \(BC = 4 \times 3 = \boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: You already know the perimeter is the distance all the way around a figure, the total you get by adding up its sides. Now we turn that into real skill.
