We will build quick formulas for rectangles and squares, work with figures whose sides are not all labelled so you have to deduce the missing ones, and find a neat trick for what the boundary does when you cut a piece away.
In the diagram above is a rectangle 12 units wide and 7 units tall. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the distance all the way around. A rectangle has two widths and two heights, so the shortcut is \(P = 2(l+w)\).
Add the width and the height first, then double that sum.
blocks.sol: A rectangle has two equal widths and two equal heights, so instead of adding four sides you can add one of each and double the result. $$P = 2(l+w) = 2(12+7) = 2\times 19 = 38$$ The perimeter is \(\boxed{38}\) units.

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a square with side 9. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: A square has four sides, and they are all the same length. So instead of adding four different numbers, you can add the same number four times.
That repeated addition is just multiplication. Multiply the side length by 4.
blocks.sol: A square's four sides are all equal, so its perimeter is \(4s\). With a side of 9 that gives $$P = 4 \times 9 = 36.$$ The perimeter is \(\boxed{36}\) units.

blocks.x: The figure above is a rectangle with length \(13\) and an unknown width. Its perimeter is \(40\). Find the width. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter \(2(l+w)\) is made of two lengths and two widths, so half of it is exactly one length plus one width. Start by halving \(40\).
Half the perimeter is \(\frac{40}{2}=20\), and that equals one length plus one width. You already know the length is \(13\), so subtract it out to uncover the width.
blocks.sol: The perimeter is \(2(l+w)\), two lengths and two widths together. That means half the perimeter is a single length plus a single width. Halving gives $$\frac{40}{2}=20,$$ and this \(20\) is one length plus one width. The length is \(13\), so the width is whatever is left after taking the length out of \(20\). We get \(20-13=7\), so the width is \(\boxed{7}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a square whose perimeter is \(48\). Find the length of one side. Give the number of units.
hints: A square has four equal sides, so the perimeter is \(4s\). You know the perimeter and want the side.
Undo the multiplication by 4. Divide the perimeter by 4 to get one side.
blocks.sol: A square's four sides are equal, so its perimeter is \(4s\). Here \(4s = 48\), so divide both sides by 4 to get one side. $$s = \frac{48}{4} = \boxed{12}$$ One side is 12 units.

blocks.x: Rectangle and square, forward and back. A rectangle has two lengths and two widths, so add one of each and double it, \(P = 2(l+w)\). A square is simpler still, four equal sides, so \(P = 4s\). Both run in reverse. Half the perimeter is one length plus one width, \(l+w=\frac{P}{2}\), so subtract the side you know to get the other. And one side of a square is a quarter of its perimeter, \(s=\frac{P}{4}\).
The diagram above shows an L-shaped figure with its six sides marked \(9\), \(3\), \(5\), \(3\), \(4\), and \(6\). Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter of any figure is the total distance around it, so it's just the sum of all its sides. Here every side you need is already marked.
Add the six marked lengths together, going once around the figure. \(9 + 3 + 5 + 3 + 4 + 6\).
blocks.sol: Perimeter is the distance all the way around, so add the six sides. Since every side is marked, no deducing is needed here, just total them up. $$9 + 3 + 5 + 3 + 4 + 6 = \boxed{30}$$ The perimeter is \(30\) units.

blocks.x: The figure above is an L-shaped room. Its six walls measure \(16\), \(10\), \(7\), \(4\), \(9\), and \(6\). A builder wants to run baseboard trim all the way around the floor, along every wall. Find the total length of baseboard needed, which is the perimeter of the room. Give the number of units.
hints: Baseboard runs along every wall with no gaps, so its total length is the perimeter, the sum of all six sides.
Every side is already marked on the figure above, so no side needs to be deduced. Add all six lengths together.
blocks.sol: The baseboard hugs every wall with no breaks, so the length you need is exactly the perimeter, the sum of all the sides. Here all six walls are already marked, so there is nothing hidden to work out. Just add them. $$16 + 10 + 7 + 4 + 9 + 6 = \boxed{52}$$ So the builder needs \(52\) units of baseboard.

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a staircase figure with all eight of its sides marked. The bottom and the left side are each \(6\), and the six little step sides are each \(2\). Add up every side to find the perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the sum of all eight sides. Read each length off the diagram and lay them out in one long sum before you add.

There is a shortcut worth noticing. A staircase that only steps outward wraps the same distance as the rectangle that boxes it in, so the three horizontal steps together span the same width as the \(6\) at the bottom, and the three vertical steps span the same height as the \(6\) on the left.
blocks.sol: Add every side. Going around, the marked lengths are the \(6\) at the bottom, the \(6\) up the left, and the six step sides of \(2\) each. $$6 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 6 = 24.$$ Here is why the answer comes out so clean. The three horizontal steps span the same total width as the \(6\) across the bottom, and the three vertical steps rise the same total height as the \(6\) on the left. So the staircase wraps exactly as far as the \(6\)-by-\(6\) box around it, giving \(2(6+6)=\boxed{24}\).

blocks.x: Figures with hidden sides. A composite figure, like an L, is just a set of straight sides that step in and out, and its perimeter is the sum of ALL of them. When a side is left unmarked, deduce it from the sides across from it, the horizontal pieces along the top span the same width as the bottom, and the vertical pieces up one side add to the same height as the other. So a missing length is the matching run minus the pieces you already have.

In the diagram above is a staircase figure. Only its overall width \(12\) along the bottom and its overall height \(8\) up the left side are marked; every other side is left blank. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: A staircase only steps outward, so slide each horizontal piece up and each vertical piece across until the outline snaps onto the smallest rectangle that would just contain it. Nothing about the total length changes.
That bounding rectangle is \(12\) wide and \(8\) tall, so its perimeter is \(2(W+H)\). Read off \(W\) and \(H\) from the two marked sides.
blocks.sol: The steps look like they should add extra length, but a staircase only ever steps outward. Slide every horizontal rung up to the top and every vertical riser out to the right, and the outline collapses onto its bounding box with no length lost. So the perimeter equals that of a \(12\) by \(8\) rectangle. $$P = 2(W+H) = 2(12+8) = \boxed{40}$$

blocks.x: The diagram above is a rectangle 14 by 9 with a small rectangular piece cut out of one corner. Its six sides, going around, are \(14\), \(7\), \(3\), \(2\), \(11\), and \(9\). Find the perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: A corner notch pushes the boundary in and then straight back out. The two little sides you gained replace exactly the length you gave up, so the perimeter matches the full rectangle the piece was cut from.
That full rectangle is 14 by 9, so its perimeter is \(2(14+9)\). Or add the six marked sides straight off the figure and check they agree.
blocks.sol: Cutting a corner notch never changes the perimeter. The two short sides of the notch step inward and then back out, and together they cover the same distance as the piece of the original edge they replaced. So the boundary is still as long as the full \(14\) by \(9\) rectangle, $$2(14+9) = 46.$$ Adding the six marked sides confirms it, \(14+7+3+2+11+9 = 46\), so the perimeter is \(\boxed{46}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a rectangle 12 units wide and 8 units tall, with a slot cut into its top edge 3 units wide and 2 units deep. Find the perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: Start with the plain rectangle, before the slot. Its perimeter is \(2(l+w)\), so \(2(12+8)\).

The slot's 3-unit width is already part of the top edge, so cutting it costs nothing there. What it adds are the two new vertical walls of the slot, each 2 units deep. Add twice the depth to the rectangle's perimeter.
blocks.sol: Begin with the whole rectangle, ignoring the slot. Its perimeter is $$2(12+8) = 40.$$ Now cut the slot. Its 3-unit width was already sitting on the top edge, so removing that strip and replacing it takes away 3 and puts back the same 3 along the slot's floor. The top edge length does not change. What is genuinely new are the two side walls of the slot, each 2 units deep, so the slot adds \(2\times 2 = 4\). The perimeter is \(40 + 4 = \boxed{44}\).

blocks.x: Staircases, notches, and slots. Two reshapings are worth knowing on sight. A staircase that only steps outward has the very same perimeter as the smallest box around it, \(2(W+H)\), since sliding every step out to the edge changes nothing. Cutting a corner notch also leaves the perimeter untouched, since the two new edges cover exactly the two you removed. But a slot cut into an edge is different, it adds twice its depth, because you travel down into it and back out.
The rectangle in the diagram above has length \((x+3)\) and width \(2x\), and its perimeter is \(30\). Find \(x\). Give the value of \(x\).
hints: Perimeter of a rectangle is \(2(l+w)\). Add the length and width, double it, and set that equal to \(30\).
Once you have \(2((x+3)+2x)=30\), divide both sides by \(2\) first. That leaves a simple equation in \(x\) to solve.
blocks.sol: Perimeter is \(2(l+w)\), so \(2((x+3)+2x)=30\). Divide both sides by \(2\) to get \((x+3)+2x=15\), which is \(3x+3=15\). Then \(3x=12\), so \(x=\boxed{4}\).

blocks.x: Perimeter as an equation. When the sides are written as expressions in \(x\), the perimeter is still their sum, so add the expressions just like numbers, combining like terms. A side of \(x+3\) and a side of \(2x\) together give \(3x+3\). Then, if you are told the total, set your expression equal to it and solve for \(x\), the same adding and doubling you already do, now carried out with letters.

Legend says that when Queen Dido founded the city of Carthage, she was allowed only as much land as a single oxhide could surround. So she cut the hide into one long, thin strip to make the longest boundary she could, then laid it in a great arc against the sea to enclose the most land possible. It is an ancient echo of a real idea, that for a fixed perimeter a rounded shape holds the most area.
In the diagram above is a triangle whose three sides are \(x\), \((x+2)\), and \((x+4)\), and its perimeter is \(27\). Find the LONGEST side. Give the number of units.
hints: Perimeter is the sum of all three sides, so add the three labels and set that equal to \(27\).
Combine the \(x\) terms to get a single equation in \(x\), solve it, then plug back into the longest label, \(x+4\).
blocks.sol: The perimeter is the sum of the three sides, so add the labels and set the total to \(27\).$$x + (x+2) + (x+4) = 27$$Combine like terms to get \(3x + 6 = 27\), so \(3x = 21\) and \(x = 7\). The longest side is \(x+4\), which is \(7+4 = \boxed{11}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a U-shaped figure with a deep slot cut down from the top. Its eight sides, going around, are \(12\), \(8\), \(4\), \(5\), \(4\), \(5\), \(4\), and \(8\). Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: Perimeter is the whole trip around the outside, so you just add every side you walk along. The slot doesn't change that rule. Both walls of the slot are part of the outside, so both get counted.
Add all eight numbers on the diagram. Group them however is easiest, and make sure both \(5\)-unit slot walls are in your sum.
blocks.sol: The perimeter is the length of the full trip around the outside, so add every side. The slot is the key trap here. When you cut a slot into an edge you carve two new walls going in and out of it, and you walk along both, so both \(5\)-unit walls count. Add all eight sides $$12 + 8 + 4 + 5 + 4 + 5 + 4 + 8 = 50.$$ The perimeter is \(\boxed{50}\) units.

review.x: In the diagram above is a rectangle 15 units wide and 8 units tall. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter runs all the way around, so add up all four sides. A rectangle has two widths and two heights, which is why \(2(l+w)\) works. Read the width and height off the diagram above.
review.sol: A rectangle's perimeter is the whole trip around the outside, and since opposite sides match it equals \(2(l+w)\). Reading the diagram above, the width is \(15\) and the height is \(8\), so $$2(15+8) = 2 \times 23 = \boxed{46}.$$ The perimeter is \(46\) units.

review.x: The diagram above shows a square with each side 11 units long. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: A square has four equal sides, so instead of adding \(11+11+11+11\) you can multiply one side by 4.
review.sol: A square is four copies of the same side, so its perimeter is \(4s\). With \(s = 11\), $$4 \times 11 = 44.$$ The perimeter is \(\boxed{44}\) units.

review.x: The rectangle in the diagram above is 16 units long, and its perimeter is 50. Find its width. Give the number of units.
hints: The full perimeter is two lengths plus two widths, so half the perimeter is one length plus one width. Take half of 50, then subtract the length you know.
review.sol: A rectangle's perimeter is \(2(l+w)\), so half of it is exactly one length plus one width. Half of \(50\) is \(25\), and that equals the length plus the width. The length is \(16\), so the width is $$25 - 16 = \boxed{9}.$$

review.x: The diagram above shows a square whose perimeter is \(52\). Find the length of one side. Give the number of units.
hints: A square has four equal sides, so its perimeter is \(4s\). To get \(s\), undo the multiplication by \(4\).
review.sol: All four sides of a square are equal, so the perimeter is \(4s\). Setting that equal to \(52\) and dividing by \(4\) gives the side.$$s = \frac{52}{4} = \boxed{13}$$

review.x: In the diagram above is an L-shape with its six sides marked \(8\), \(4\), \(5\), \(3\), \(3\), and \(7\). Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: An L-shape has no shortcut formula, so its perimeter is just the sum of every side. Add all six marked lengths, once around the figure.
review.sol: The perimeter of any figure is the total distance around it, so add up all six sides marked in the diagram. $$8+4+5+3+3+7=\boxed{30}$$ Walking once around the L and adding each side as you pass it gives \(30\) units.

review.x: In the diagram above is a staircase figure with its six sides marked \(8\), \(3\), \(4\), \(3\), \(4\), and \(6\). Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the whole way around, so add up every side you see marked on the figure.
review.sol: Perimeter is the total distance around the figure, so add all six marked sides. $$8+3+4+3+4+6 = \boxed{28}$$ The perimeter is \(28\) units.

review.x: The staircase in the diagram above steps outward from bottom to top, and only two lengths are marked: the total width \(10\) along the bottom and the total height \(7\) up the left side. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: Trace all the horizontal steps and they still span the full width of \(10\); trace all the vertical steps and they still climb the full height of \(7\). Compare that to the smallest rectangle the staircase fits inside.
review.sol: A staircase that only steps outward has the same perimeter as its bounding box, the smallest rectangle it fits inside. Every horizontal piece of the top edge, added up, spans the same \(10\) as the bottom, and every vertical piece down the right side, added up, climbs the same \(7\) as the left. So the total path around the staircase matches the path around the \(10\) by \(7\) rectangle. $$P = 2(10 + 7) = 2(17) = \boxed{34}$$ You never needed the individual step sizes. As long as the figure only steps out, they rearrange into the sides of the bounding box.

review.x: The figure above is a 12-by-8 rectangle with a small notch cut out of one corner. Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: Cutting a corner notch swaps one right angle for a little step, but the sides you removed reappear as the sides of the notch. Think about whether the total distance around actually changes.
review.sol: Cutting a notch out of a corner does not change the perimeter. The two short segments removed from the outer edges are exactly replaced by the two segments of the notch, so the trip around the boundary is the same length as around the full rectangle. That means you can ignore the notch and use \(2(l+w)\) on the original 12-by-8 rectangle. $$2(12+8) = \boxed{40}$$

review.x: The figure above is an 11-by-7 rectangle with a slot 2 units wide and 3 units deep cut straight into its top edge. Find the perimeter of this figure. Give the number of units.
hints: Start from the full rectangle, then think about what the slot does to the boundary. The slot's two vertical walls both get traced, so it adds twice its depth, not its width.
review.sol: Cutting a slot into an edge adds twice its depth, since you now trace both walls going down and back up. The full rectangle is \(2(11+7) = 36\), and the slot adds \(2\times 3 = 6\). $$36 + 6 = \boxed{42}$$ The width of the slot changes nothing here, because the top edge you removed is exactly replaced by the slot's floor. The perimeter is \(42\) units.

review.x: In the diagram above is a rectangle whose length is \((x+5)\) and whose width is \(3x\). Its perimeter is \(42\). Find the value of \(x\). Give the value of \(x\).
hints: A rectangle's perimeter is \(2(l+w)\), so the length and width together make half the perimeter. Set \((x+5)+3x\) equal to \(21\) and solve.
review.sol: The perimeter is \(2(l+w)\), so one length plus one width is half of it, \(21\). That gives $$(x+5)+3x = 21.$$ Combine the \(x\) terms to get \(4x + 5 = 21\), so \(4x = 16\) and \(x = \boxed{4}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is an isosceles triangle whose sides are labelled \(x\), \(2x\), and \(2x\), and its perimeter is \(40\). Find the length of the longest side. Give the number of units.
hints: Add the three side labels to get the perimeter in terms of \(x\), set that equal to \(40\), and solve for \(x\). The longest side is the \(2x\) one.
review.sol: The perimeter is the sum of all three sides, so \(x + 2x + 2x = 5x\). Setting that equal to the given perimeter gives $$5x = 40,\qquad x = 8.$$ The longest side is \(2x = 2(8) = \boxed{16}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is an L-shaped patio. Its walls, going around, measure \(15, 9, 6, 4, 9,\) and \(5\) units. Find the perimeter of the patio. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is the whole trip around the edge, so add up every wall you walk along.
review.sol: Perimeter is the total distance around the figure, so add all six walls in order. $$15+9+6+4+9+5 = \boxed{48}$$ The patio's perimeter is \(48\) units.

review.x: The diagram above shows a U-shape with its sides marked \(14, 9, 5, 6, 4, 6, 5, 9\). Find its perimeter. Give the number of units.
hints: The perimeter is just the total distance around the figure, so add up every side you see marked.
review.sol: The perimeter of any figure is the sum of all its sides, so read the eight marked lengths off the diagram and add them up. $$14+9+5+6+4+6+5+9 = \boxed{58}$$ The perimeter is \(58\) units.

blocks.x: Perimeter measured the boundary, the walk around the edge. Area measures the surface inside, the flat space a figure actually covers. We count that space in little squares. Start with a rectangle and see why its squares line up so neatly, then slice that rectangle into a triangle, and finally build up figures made of several shapes stitched together.
In the diagram above is a rectangle 8 units wide and 5 units tall. Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: A rectangle's area is its length times its width. Read both side lengths off the diagram above.
Multiply the width by the height, \(8 \times 5\).
blocks.sol: The area of a rectangle is its length times its width. Reading the two sides off the diagram above, $$A = l \times w = 8 \times 5 = \boxed{40}.$$ The area is 40 square units.

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a square with side 7. Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: A square's area is its side times itself, \(s^2\). Read the side length off the diagram.
The side is 7, so square it. \(7 \times 7\).
blocks.sol: A square's area is the side squared, \(A = s^2\). Here the side is 7, so $$A = 7^2 = \boxed{49}.$$ The area is 49 square units.

blocks.x: Area, and the area of a rectangle. Area is the amount of surface a flat figure covers, measured by how many unit squares fit inside it. A unit square is one unit on each side, so area is always given in square units, written with a small \(2\), as in \(\text{cm}^2\). Fill a rectangle with those squares and they line up in neat rows and columns, one column for each unit of width and one row for each unit of height, so the count is just length times width, \(A = l \times w\). A square has equal sides, so its area is a side times itself, \(A = s^2\).
In the diagram above is a rectangle with length \(9\) and an unknown width. Its area is \(72\). Find the width. Give the number of units.
hints: A rectangle's area is length times width. You know the area and the length, so you're missing one factor.
To undo the multiplication, divide the area by the length you know.
blocks.sol: The area of a rectangle is \(l \times w\), so \(9 \times w = 72\). To find the missing width, divide the area by the length. $$w = \frac{72}{9} = 8$$ The width is \(\boxed{8}\).

blocks.x: The diagram above is a square whose area is \(81\). Find the length of one side. Give the number of units.
hints: A square's area is its side times itself, so the side is the number that gives \(81\) when multiplied by itself.
Ask which number times itself is \(81\). That is \(\sqrt{81}\).
blocks.sol: A square's area is the side times itself, so to undo that we take the square root of the area. $$s = \sqrt{81} = 9$$ The side is \(\boxed{9}\).

blocks.x: Working backward from the area. The area formula runs in reverse. If you know the area and one side of a rectangle, divide to find the missing side, since \(l \times w = A\) means \(w = \frac{A}{l}\). A square is quicker still. Its area is a side squared, so the side is the number that multiplies by itself to give the area, its square root, \(s = \sqrt{A}\).
The diagram above shows a right triangle. The small square marks the right angle, and its two legs measure \(8\) and \(6\). Find the area of the triangle. Give the number of square units.
hints: The two legs of a right triangle meet at the right angle, so they serve as the base and the height. Use \(A = \frac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times \text{height}\).
Multiply the two legs together, then take half. Half of \(8\times 6\) is the area.
blocks.sol: A triangle's area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times \text{height}\). In a right triangle the two legs meet at the right angle, so they act as the base and the height. Here the legs are \(8\) and \(6\), so $$A = \frac{1}{2}\times 8 \times 6 = \frac{1}{2}\times 48 = \boxed{24}.$$ The area is \(24\) square units.

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a triangle with base \(10\) and height \(6\) (the dashed line is the height). Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: For a triangle, area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times \text{height}\). Read the base and the height off the diagram.
Multiply the base by the height first, then take half of that.
blocks.sol: The area of a triangle is \(\frac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times \text{height}\). The base is \(10\) and the dashed height is \(6\), so $$A = \frac{1}{2}\times 10 \times 6 = \boxed{30}.$$

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a triangle with base \(14\) and height \(9\). Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: A triangle's area is half of the base times the height. Which length is the base, and which is the height straight across from it?
Multiply the base by the height, then take half of that product.
blocks.sol: A triangle covers half the rectangle you could box around it, so its area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times \text{height}\). Reading the base \(14\) and the height \(9\) off the figure above, $$A = \frac{1}{2}\times 14 \times 9 = \boxed{63}.$$ The area is \(63\) square units.

blocks.x: Area of a triangle. Slice any rectangle along a diagonal and you get two identical right triangles, so each covers exactly half the rectangle. That is why a triangle has area half of base times height, \(A = \frac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times \text{height}\). This holds for every triangle, not just right ones. Pick any side as the base; the height is the straight-across distance from that base to the opposite corner, meeting the base at a right angle. The triangle fills exactly half the rectangle that is base wide and height tall.
The diagram above is an L-shaped figure with the sides marked \(9\), \(3\), \(5\), \(3\), \(4\), \(6\). Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: Cut the L with one horizontal line so it becomes two rectangles stacked on each other. Find the width and height of each piece from the marked sides.
Work out each rectangle's area with \(l \times w\), then add the two areas together.
blocks.sol: Split the L into two rectangles and add their areas. The bottom rectangle is \(9\) wide and \(3\) tall, so it holds \(9 \times 3 = 27\). The top rectangle is \(4\) wide and \(3\) tall, so it holds \(4 \times 3 = 12\). Adding them gives $$27 + 12 = \boxed{39}.$$ You could also take the full \(9 \times 6\) box and subtract the \(5 \times 3\) notch, \(54 - 15 = 39\), which lands on the same answer.

blocks.x: The figure above is a \(10\) by \(8\) rectangle with a right-triangular corner sliced off, the cut having legs \(6\) and \(4\) (shown dashed). Find the area of the shape that remains. Give the number of square units.
hints: Start with the whole rectangle before anything was cut. Its area is \(10\times 8\).
The removed corner is a right triangle with legs \(6\) and \(4\), so its area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times 6\times 4\). Subtract that from the rectangle.
blocks.sol: Take the full rectangle and remove the triangle that was cut away. The rectangle covers \(10\times 8 = 80\) square units. The sliced corner is a right triangle whose legs \(6\) and \(4\) act as base and height, so it covers \(\frac{1}{2}\times 6\times 4 = 12\) square units. What remains is the rectangle minus that corner. $$10\times 8 - \frac{1}{2}\times 6\times 4 = 80 - 12 = \boxed{68}$$ The area of the remaining shape is \(68\) square units.

blocks.x: The diagram above shows a rectangular frame: a \(10\) by \(8\) outer rectangle with a \(6\) by \(4\) rectangular hole cut out of the middle. The shaded part is what remains. Find the shaded area. Give the number of square units.
hints: Find the whole outer rectangle's area first as if the hole were not there, then deal with the hole.
The shaded region is the outer rectangle with the inner rectangle removed, so subtract the hole's area from the outer area.
blocks.sol: Take the big rectangle and subtract the cut-out piece. The outer rectangle has area \(10\times 8 = 80\), and the hole has area \(6\times 4 = 24\). Subtract to get what remains, $$10\times 8 - 6\times 4 = 80 - 24 = \boxed{56}.$$ The shaded area is \(56\) square units.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, a \(12\) by \(6\) region is to be covered by tiles that are \(2\) by \(3\), like the shaded one. How many tiles are needed? Give the number of tiles.
hints: The number of tiles is the region's area divided by one tile's area, so first find each area.
The region covers \(12\times 6\) square units and one tile covers \(2\times 3\). Divide.
blocks.sol: Tiling a region means the number of tiles is the region's area divided by one tile's area. The region covers \(12\times 6 = 72\) square units and each tile covers \(2\times 3 = 6\). Divide to get the count. $$\frac{12\times 6}{2\times 3} = \frac{72}{6} = \boxed{12}$$ So \(12\) tiles are needed.

blocks.x: Areas that come in pieces. A figure stitched from several shapes can be handled two ways. Split it into rectangles and triangles, find each area, and ADD them up; or start from a whole rectangle around the figure and SUBTRACT the piece that was cut away. Both give the same answer, so pick whichever is less work. And to tile a region, divide the area of the region by the area of one tile to get the number of tiles.

In the diagram above is an L-shaped room floor with the marked sides \(12\), \(6\), \(4\), \(3\), \(8\), and \(9\). Find its area so you know how much flooring to buy. Give the number of square units.
hints: An L-shape is a full rectangle with one corner cut out. Picture the whole \(12\times 9\) rectangle it would be if the notch were filled in.
Find the big rectangle's area, then find the area of the missing \(4\times 3\) corner, and subtract the missing piece from the whole.
blocks.sol: Fill in the notch and treat the shape as one big rectangle, then subtract the corner that was cut away. The full rectangle is \(12\) by \(9\), so its area is \(12\times 9 = 108\). The missing corner is \(4\) by \(3\), an area of \(4\times 3 = 12\). Subtract the cut-out from the whole. $$108 - 12 = \boxed{96}$$ The floor covers \(96\) square units.

blocks.x: The figure above is a house shape: a rectangle \(10\) wide and \(6\) tall, with a triangular roof of height \(4\) sitting on top. The dashed line is where the roof meets the wall. Find the total area of the whole house. Give the number of square units.
hints: The dashed line splits the house into two shapes you already know, a rectangle below and a triangle above. Find each area on its own.
The roof's base is the same \(10\) as the rectangle's width, and its height is \(4\). A triangle's area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times \text{height}\). Add that to the rectangle to finish.
blocks.sol: Split the house at the dashed line and add the two pieces. The rectangle below has area \(10\times 6 = 60\). The roof is a triangle with base \(10\) and height \(4\), so its area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times 10 \times 4 = 20\). Add them for the total, $$60 + 20 = \boxed{80}.$$

blocks.x: An acre started as a day's work, not a measurement. It was roughly the strip of land a farmer with one ox team could plough in a single day, so its size grew out of how much ground the animals could cover before sundown rather than any tidy formula. A long field took less turning of the team than a wide one, so acres came out long and narrow. Only much later was the acre pinned down to exactly \(43{,}560\) square feet, which is why such an ordinary patch of ground hides such an odd-looking number.

review.x: In the diagram above is a rectangle 11 wide and 6 tall. Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: Area of a rectangle is width times height. Multiply the two side lengths marked on the figure.
review.sol: The area of a rectangle is its width times its height. Multiply the two lengths marked on the diagram, \(11\times 6 = 66\). So the area is \(\boxed{66}\) square units.

review.x: The diagram above shows a square with side 8. Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: A square's area is its side times itself, \(s^2\). Square the 8 marked in the figure.
review.sol: A square's area is \(s^2\), the side multiplied by itself. The side here is 8, so $$8^2 = 8 \times 8 = \boxed{64}.$$ The area is 64 square units.

review.x: The rectangle in the diagram above has length \(15\) and an unknown width, and its area is \(90\) square units. Find the missing width. Give the number of units.
hints: Area is length times width, so the width is the area divided by the length you know.
review.sol: The area of a rectangle is length times width, so the width is what the length must be multiplied by to reach the area. Divide the area by the length.$$\frac{90}{15} = \boxed{6}$$So the missing width is \(6\).

review.x: The diagram above shows a square with an area of \(100\) square units. Find the length of one side. Give the number of units.
hints: The area of a square is \(s^2\), so the side is the number that squares to \(100\). That is \(\sqrt{100}\).
review.sol: A square with side \(s\) has area \(s^2\). Here \(s^2 = 100\), so \(s = \sqrt{100}\). Since \(10 \times 10 = 100\), the side is $$s = \sqrt{100} = \boxed{10}.$$

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle. Its two legs, the sides that meet at the square corner, are 12 and 5. Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: A right triangle is just half of a rectangle, so its area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times \text{height}\). The two legs meet at the square corner, which means one leg is the base and the other is the height straight across from it. Take half the product of those two legs.
review.sol: The two legs meet at the right angle, so one serves as the base and the other as the height measured straight across from it. Area is half the base times the height. $$\frac{1}{2}\times 12 \times 5$$ Multiply the legs first, \(12 \times 5 = 60\), then take half. $$\frac{1}{2}\times 60 = 30$$ So the area is \(\boxed{30}\) square units.

review.x: The diagram above shows a triangle with base 16 and height 5. Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: A triangle covers half the rectangle it fits inside, so its area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times \text{base}\times \text{height}\). Read the base and height off the diagram and put them in.
review.sol: A triangle's area is half its base times its height. The diagram gives a base of 16 and a height of 5, so $$\frac{1}{2}\times 16 \times 5 = 40.$$ The area is \(\boxed{40}\) square units.

review.x: The diagram above shows a triangle with base 9 and height 8. Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: A triangle's area is half of base times height. Read the base and the height off the figure and multiply, then take half.
review.sol: A triangle covers half of the rectangle that boxes it in, so its area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times\text{base}\times\text{height}\). Here the base is 9 and the height is 8, so $$\frac{1}{2}\times 9 \times 8 = \frac{72}{2} = 36.$$ The area is \(\boxed{36}\) square units.

review.x: The diagram above shows an L-shaped figure with sides \(8\), \(4\), \(5\), \(3\), \(3\), \(7\). Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: Cut the L into two rectangles, or picture the full \(8\times 7\) box with a rectangular notch cut out of it. Find the area of each piece from the marked lengths, then combine.
review.sol: Split the L into two rectangles. The wide part is \(8\times 4 = 32\), and the narrow part below it is \(3\times 3 = 9\). Add them. $$32 + 9 = \boxed{41}$$ You can check it the other way. The whole \(8\times 7\) box is \(56\), and the missing notch is \(5\times 3 = 15\), so \(56 - 15 = 41\). The area is \(41\) square units.

review.x: The diagram above shows a rectangle 9 by 8 with a right-triangular corner sliced off, the two legs of the cut measuring 6 and 4. Find the area of the shape that remains. Give the number of square units.
hints: Start with the whole rectangle, then subtract the triangular corner that was removed. The corner is a right triangle, so its two legs are its base and height.
review.sol: Take the full rectangle and subtract the triangle that was cut away. The rectangle is \(9\times 8 = 72\). The removed corner is a right triangle with legs 6 and 4, so its area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times 6\times 4 = 12\). What is left is $$72 - 12 = \boxed{60}$$

review.x: The diagram above shows a \(10\) by \(8\) region covered by \(2\) by \(2\) square tiles, with no gaps or overlaps. How many tiles cover the whole region? Give the number of tiles.
hints: The number of tiles is the region's area divided by one tile's area. Find each area first, then divide.
review.sol: The number of tiles is the region's area divided by the area of one tile. The region is \(10\times 8 = 80\) square units, and each tile is \(2\times 2 = 4\) square units. Divide to get the count. $$\frac{80}{4} = \boxed{20}$$ So it takes \(20\) tiles.

review.x: The diagram above shows a rectangular frame: a \(12\) by \(9\) rectangle with an \(8\) by \(5\) rectangular hole cut out of it. Find the shaded area of the frame. Give the number of square units.
hints: Find the area of the full \(12\) by \(9\) rectangle, then subtract the area of the hole.
review.sol: The shaded frame is the big rectangle with the hole taken out, so take the big area and subtract the hole. The outer rectangle is \(12\times 9 = 108\), and the hole is \(8\times 5 = 40\). Subtracting gives $$108 - 40 = \boxed{68}.$$

review.x: The diagram above shows an L-shaped patio with sides \(11\), \(6\), \(3\), \(2\), \(8\), \(8\). Find its area. Give the number of square units.
hints: Picture the full \(11\times 8\) rectangle, then notice a small corner is missing. Find the big area and the corner's area, then take one away from the other.
review.sol: Fill in the notch to make a full rectangle, then subtract the piece you added. The whole rectangle is \(11\times 8 = 88\), and the missing corner is \(3\times 2 = 6\). So the patio is $$88 - 6 = \boxed{82}.$$ The area is 82 square units.

review.x: The figure above is a house shape, a rectangle \(8\) wide and \(5\) tall with a triangular roof of height \(3\) on top. Find the total area. Give the number of square units.
hints: Split the house into its two pieces. The rectangle below has area \(l\times w\), and the triangular roof has area \(\frac{1}{2}\times\text{base}\times\text{height}\). Find each, then add.
review.sol: Split the house into the rectangle and the triangular roof, then add the two areas. The rectangle is \(8\times 5 = 40\). The roof has base \(8\) and height \(3\), so its area is \(\frac{1}{2}\times 8\times 3 = 12\). Adding them, $$40 + 12 = \boxed{52}$$ The total area is \(52\) square units.

blocks.x: Every shape so far has had straight sides you could count and add. A circle has none. Yet it still has a length running all the way around it and a flat space filling its inside, a perimeter and an area, just like the others. Measuring them turns up one famous number, \(\pi\), that hides inside every circle ever drawn. Once you have hold of it, you can find the distance around and the space within for any circle at all.
In the diagram above is a circle whose radius is \(8\). Find its diameter. Give the number of units.
hints: The diameter is twice the radius, \(d = 2r\). Read the radius off the diagram above.
The radius is \(8\), so double it.
blocks.sol: The diameter is twice the radius, \(d = 2r\). The diagram above marks the radius as \(8\), so $$d = 2 \times 8 = 16.$$ The diameter is \(\boxed{16}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a circle with diameter \(10\). Its circumference is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: The circumference of a circle is \(C = \pi d\), where \(d\) is the diameter. Read the diameter off the diagram above.
The diameter is \(10\), so the circumference is \(10\) times \(\pi\). The whole number you report is the one sitting in front of \(\pi\).
blocks.sol: The circumference is pi times the diameter. Read the diameter straight off the diagram above, it is \(10\), and plug it in. $$C = \pi d = \pi \times 10 = 10\pi$$ So the circumference is \(10\pi\), and the whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{10}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a circle with radius \(7\). Its circumference is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: Circumference is \(C = 2\pi r\). Read the radius off the diagram above and put it in.
With \(r = 7\) you get \(2 \times 7 \times \pi\). Work out the whole number in front of \(\pi\).
blocks.sol: The circumference of a circle is \(C = 2\pi r\). The radius in the diagram above is \(7\), so $$C = 2 \times 7 \times \pi = 14\pi.$$ That is a whole number times \(\pi\), and the whole number in front is \(\boxed{14}\).

blocks.x: Radius, diameter, and circumference. Every point of a circle sits the same distance from the center, and that distance is the radius $r$. A straight line all the way across through the center is the diameter, just two radii laid end to end, so $d = 2r$ (and $r = \frac{d}{2}$). Roll a circle along one full turn and the distance it covers is its circumference. That distance is always the same multiple of the diameter, no matter the size, and that multiple is the number $\pi$ (about $3.14$), the same for every circle in the world. So $C = \pi d = 2\pi r$. With a whole-number radius the circumference lands as a whole number times $\pi$, like $14\pi$, and we leave it in that form and give the whole number in front.
In the diagram above is a circle with radius \(6\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: Area of a circle is \(A = \pi r^2\). Read the radius off the diagram above and square it.
With \(r = 6\) you get \(6^2\). The whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is that value.
blocks.sol: The area of a circle is \(A = \pi r^2\). Read the radius \(6\) off the diagram above and square it. $$A = \pi \times 6^2 = 36\pi$$ The area is \(36\pi\), so the whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{36}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a circle with diameter \(14\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: The area formula uses the radius, not the diameter, so first find the radius from \(d = 2r\).
With the radius in hand, use \(A = \pi r^2\) and read off the whole number sitting in front of \(\pi\).
blocks.sol: The area formula needs the radius, so start from the diameter. Since \(d = 2r\), the radius is \(r = \frac{14}{2} = 7\). Now apply the area of a circle, \(A = \pi r^2\). $$A = \pi \cdot 7^2 = 49\pi.$$ The area is \(49\pi\), so the whole number is \(\boxed{49}\).

blocks.x: Area of a circle. Slice a circle into many thin wedges and lay them in a row, alternating point up and point down. They line up into a shape very close to a parallelogram with base $\pi r$ (half the way around) and height $r$. Its area is base times height, $\pi r \times r$, so the area of a circle is $A = \pi r^2$. Like the circumference, this lands as a whole number times $\pi$, and you give that whole number — an area of $36\pi$ is answered $36$. If you are handed the diameter, halve it to get $r$ first.
In the diagram above is a circle whose circumference is \(26\pi\), with the radius marked by a question mark. Find the radius. Give the number of units.
hints: The circumference of a circle is \(C = 2\pi r\). Set that equal to \(26\pi\).
From \(2\pi r = 26\pi\), cancel the \(\pi\) on both sides to get \(2r = 26\), then divide by \(2\).
blocks.sol: This is a reverse problem, so we work backward from the circumference. The rule is circumference equals \(2\pi r\). We know the circumference is \(26\pi\), so $$2\pi r = 26\pi.$$ Cancel the \(\pi\) on both sides to get \(2r = 26\), then divide by \(2\) to find \(r = 13\). The radius is \(\boxed{13}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a circle whose area is \(64\pi\). Find the radius. Give the number of units.
hints: The area of a circle is \(A = \pi r^2\). Set that equal to the area you are given, \(64\pi\), and cancel the \(\pi\) from both sides.
You are left with \(r^2 = 64\). Ask which number times itself gives \(64\).
blocks.sol: This is a reverse problem, so we start from the area formula and work back to the radius. The area is \(A = \pi r^2\), and here it equals \(64\pi\), so $$\pi r^2 = 64\pi.$$ Cancel the \(\pi\) on both sides to get \(r^2 = 64\). The number that squares to \(64\) is \(8\), so \(r = \boxed{8}\).

blocks.x: Working backward. The circle formulas run in reverse too. Given a circumference or an area written as a whole number times $\pi$, set the formula equal to it and cancel the $\pi$ that sits on both sides. For a circumference, $2\pi r = 20\pi$ becomes $2r = 20$, so $r = 10$ — undo the doubling. For an area, $\pi r^2 = 49\pi$ becomes $r^2 = 49$, so $r = 7$ — take the square root.
In the diagram above is a semicircle with radius \(8\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: A semicircle is half a circle, so its area is half of a full circle's area, \(\frac{1}{2}\pi r^2\). Read the radius off the diagram above.
Square the radius \(8\) to get \(64\), then take half of \(64\pi\). The answer is the whole number left in front of \(\pi\).
blocks.sol: A semicircle is half a circle, so its area is half of the full circle's \(\pi r^2\). With radius \(8\), $$A = \frac{1}{2}\pi r^2 = \frac{1}{2}\times \pi \times 64 = 32\pi.$$ We leave the answer in terms of \(\pi\), so the area is \(32\pi\) and the whole number is \(\boxed{32}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a quarter circle with radius \(10\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: A quarter circle is a fourth of a full circle, so its area is \(\frac{1}{4}\pi r^2\). Read the radius off the diagram.
Square the radius first, then take a fourth of it. With \(r = 10\) you get \(r^2 = 100\).
blocks.sol: A quarter circle is one fourth of a full circle, so its area is \(\frac{1}{4}\pi r^2\). With \(r = 10\), $$A = \frac{1}{4}\pi r^2 = \frac{1}{4}\times \pi \times 100 = 25\pi.$$ The area is \(25\pi\), so the whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{25}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a ring, the shaded space between an outer circle of radius \(10\) and an inner circle of radius \(6\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: The ring is what's left when you cut the small circle out of the big one, so its area is \(\pi R^2 - \pi r^2\) with \(R = 10\) and \(r = 6\).
Find each area on its own first. The big circle is \(\pi \times 10^2\) and the small one is \(\pi \times 6^2\), then subtract.
blocks.sol: A ring is the big circle with the small circle removed, so its area is the difference of the two areas. The big circle has area \(\pi \times 10^2 = 100\pi\) and the small circle has area \(\pi \times 6^2 = 36\pi\). Subtract to get what's left in the ring. $$100\pi - 36\pi = 64\pi$$ The area is \(64\pi\), so the whole number is \(\boxed{64}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above are two pizzas, a small one of radius \(6\) and a large one of radius \(12\). How many times as much pizza (area) is the large one? Give the number.
hints: Area grows with the radius squared, since \(A = \pi r^2\). Start by comparing the radii: how many times bigger is the large radius than the small one?
The radius goes from \(6\) to \(12\), so it doubles. When the radius is multiplied by a number, the area is multiplied by that number squared.
blocks.sol: The area of a circle is \(A = \pi r^2\), so area depends on the radius squared. Going from radius \(6\) to radius \(12\), the radius doubles, and squaring that scale factor gives $$2^2 = 4.$$ You can check it directly. The small pizza has area \(\pi \times 6^2 = 36\pi\) and the large one has \(\pi \times 12^2 = 144\pi\), and \(144\pi\) is \(4\) times \(36\pi\). So the large pizza is \(\boxed{4}\) times as much.

blocks.x: Parts of a circle, and scaling. A slice of a circle keeps the same $\pi r^2$, scaled by how much of the whole it is: a semicircle is $\frac{1}{2}\pi r^2$ and a quarter circle is $\frac{1}{4}\pi r^2$. A ring is the flat band between an outer circle of radius $R$ and an inner circle of radius $r$, found by removing the small circle from the big one, $\pi R^2 - \pi r^2$. And because area depends on the radius squared, growing the radius by a factor multiplies the area by the square of that factor — double the radius and the area becomes $2^2 = 4$ times as big, triple it and $3^2 = 9$ times.

The number \(\pi\) never ends and never falls into a repeating pattern. Its digits run on forever with no rhythm, which makes it an irrational number, and computers have now chased it out to trillions of places past the decimal point. It is famous enough to have its own holiday. Every March 14, written 3/14 in the United States, is celebrated as Pi Day, and a few devoted fans memorize thousands of its digits just for the fun of it.

In the diagram above, the small circular garden has radius \(10\) and needs \(3000\) seeds to fill it. The larger garden has three times that radius. How many seeds does it need to fill? Give the number of seeds.
hints: Area grows with the square of the radius. If the radius is multiplied by \(3\), the area is multiplied by \(3^2\).
Work out \(3^2\), then multiply the number of seeds for the small garden by that factor.
blocks.sol: Seeds needed match the area, and the area of a circle depends on the radius squared. Tripling the radius multiplies the area by $$3^2 = 9.$$ So the larger garden needs \(9\) times as many seeds as the small one, $$9 \times 3000 = 27000.$$ The larger garden needs \(\boxed{27000}\) seeds.

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a dartboard of three circles with radii \(4\), \(6\), and \(9\). Find the area of the outer ring, the shaded space between the radius-\(6\) circle and the radius-\(9\) circle. That area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: A ring is a big circle with a smaller one punched out of the middle. Its area is the outer circle's area minus the inner circle's area, \(\pi R^2 - \pi r^2\). Here the innermost radius-\(4\) circle is a distraction; the outer ring only uses \(R = 9\) and \(r = 6\).
Work out each area on its own first. The big circle has area \(\pi \times 9^2\), and the circle you subtract has area \(\pi \times 6^2\). Then subtract the whole numbers in front of \(\pi\).
blocks.sol: The outer ring is the space left when you take the radius-\(9\) circle and remove the radius-\(6\) circle from inside it. So its area is outer area minus inner area, and the radius-\(4\) circle plays no part. The big circle has area \(\pi \times 9^2 = 81\pi\), and the circle we remove has area \(\pi \times 6^2 = 36\pi\). Subtracting, $$81\pi - 36\pi = 45\pi.$$ The ring's area is \(45\pi\), so the whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{45}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a circle with radius \(11\). Its diameter is a length you can read off. Find the diameter. Give the number of units.
hints: The diameter is twice the radius, so \(d = 2r\). Double the \(11\) marked in the diagram.
review.sol: The diameter runs all the way across a circle, and it is exactly twice the radius, so \(d = 2r\). Here the radius is \(11\), so $$d = 2 \times 11 = 22.$$ The diameter is \(\boxed{22}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a circle with diameter \(8\). Its circumference is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: The circumference of a circle is \(C = \pi d\), and the diameter is marked in the diagram above.
review.sol: The circumference is the diameter times \(\pi\). Read the diameter off the diagram above, \(d = 8\), and use $$C = \pi d = 8\pi.$$ So the whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{8}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a circle with radius \(9\). Its circumference is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: Circumference is \(C = 2\pi r\). Double the radius to get the whole number in front of \(\pi\).
review.sol: The circumference of a circle is \(C = 2\pi r\). Here the radius is \(9\), so $$C = 2\pi \times 9 = 18\pi.$$ Leaving the answer in terms of \(\pi\), the whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{18}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a circle with radius \(5\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: The area of a circle is \(A = \pi r^2\). Square the radius, then read off the whole number sitting in front of \(\pi\).
review.sol: The area of a circle is \(A = \pi r^2\). Read the radius \(5\) off the diagram above and square it. $$A = \pi \times 5^2 = 25\pi$$ The area is \(25\pi\), so the whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{25}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a circle with diameter \(20\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: The diameter is twice the radius, so \(r = \tfrac{20}{2} = 10\). Then the area is \(\pi r^2\).
review.sol: The diameter is \(20\), and the radius is half of that, so \(r = 10\). Area is \(\pi r^2\), so $$A = \pi \times 10^2 = 100\pi.$$ We leave the answer in terms of \(\pi\) and give just the whole number in front, so the answer is \(\boxed{100}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a circle whose circumference is \(30\pi\). Find its radius. Give the number of units.
hints: The circumference is \(2\pi r\). Set \(2\pi r = 30\pi\) and solve for \(r\).
review.sol: This runs the circumference formula backward. The circumference is \(2\pi r\), and here it equals \(30\pi\), so $$2\pi r = 30\pi.$$ Divide both sides by \(2\pi\) to get \(r = 15\). The radius is \(\boxed{15}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a circle whose area is \(49\pi\). Find its radius. Give the number of units.
hints: Area is \(\pi r^2\). Set \(\pi r^2 = 49\pi\), cancel the \(\pi\), and ask what number squared gives \(49\).
review.sol: The area of a circle is \(\pi r^2\). Here that equals \(49\pi\), so $$\pi r^2 = 49\pi.$$ Divide both sides by \(\pi\) to get \(r^2 = 49\). The number that squares to \(49\) is \(7\), so the radius is \(\boxed{7}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a semicircle with radius \(10\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: A semicircle is half a circle, so its area is \(\frac{1}{2}\pi r^2\). Start by squaring the radius.
review.sol: A semicircle is half a full circle, so its area is half of \(\pi r^2\). With \(r = 10\) the radius squared is \(r^2 = 100\), so $$A = \frac{1}{2}\pi r^2 = \frac{1}{2}\times 100\pi = 50\pi.$$ The area is \(50\pi\), so the whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{50}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a quarter circle with radius \(8\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: A quarter circle is a fourth of a whole circle, so its area is \(\frac{1}{4}\pi r^2\). Read the radius off the diagram above and square it first.
review.sol: A quarter circle covers a fourth of the whole circle, so its area is \(\frac{1}{4}\pi r^2\). Reading the radius off the diagram above, \(r = 8\), so \(r^2 = 64\) and $$A = \frac{1}{4}\pi r^2 = \frac{1}{4}\times 64\pi = 16\pi.$$ We leave the answer in terms of \(\pi\), so the whole number in front of \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{16}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a ring between an outer circle of radius \(13\) and an inner circle of radius \(5\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: The ring's area is the big circle's area minus the small circle's hole, \(\pi R^2 - \pi r^2\). Work out each \(\pi r^2\) and subtract.
review.sol: A ring is the big disk with the small disk punched out, so its area is the outer area minus the inner area. Using \(A = \pi r^2\) for each, $$\pi \times 13^2 - \pi \times 5^2 = 169\pi - 25\pi = 144\pi.$$ The area is \(144\pi\), so the whole number multiplying \(\pi\) is \(\boxed{144}\).

review.x: In the diagram above are two circles, one with radius \(5\) and one with radius \(10\). The radius of the larger circle is twice the radius of the smaller. How many times as big as the smaller circle's area is the larger circle's area? Give the number.
hints: Area is \(\pi r^2\), so doubling the radius multiplies the area by \(2^2\). Work out \(2^2\).
review.sol: Area depends on the radius squared, \(A = \pi r^2\). The small circle has area \(\pi \cdot 5^2 = 25\pi\) and the large one has area \(\pi \cdot 10^2 = 100\pi\), and \(100\pi\) is \(4\) times \(25\pi\). Doubling the radius doesn't double the area, it multiplies it by \(2^2\).$$2^2 = \boxed{4}$$

review.x: In the diagram above, a small circular flower bed of radius \(7\) needs \(150\) bags of soil to fill it. The larger bed beside it has twice the radius. How many bags of soil does the larger bed need? Give the number of bags.
hints: Doubling the radius does not double the area. Since area depends on \(r^2\), doubling the radius multiplies the area by \(2^2 = 4\), so the larger bed needs \(4\) times as many bags.
review.sol: The area of a circle is \(\pi r^2\), so it grows with the square of the radius. Doubling the radius multiplies the area by \(2^2 = 4\). The larger bed covers \(4\) times the area, so it needs \(4\) times the soil. $$4 \times 150 = 600$$ The larger bed needs \(\boxed{600}\) bags.

review.x: In the diagram above is a dartboard of three circles with radii \(5\), \(8\), and \(12\). Find the area of the outer ring, the region between the circle of radius \(8\) and the circle of radius \(12\). Its area is a whole number times \(\pi\). Find that whole number. Give the whole number multiplying \(\pi\).
hints: The ring is the big circle with the smaller one punched out, so its area is \(\pi R^2 - \pi r^2\). Here \(R = 12\) and \(r = 8\).
review.sol: A ring is one circle with a smaller one taken out of it, so its area is the big area minus the small area, \(\pi R^2 - \pi r^2\). The outer edge is at radius \(12\) and the inner edge is at radius \(8\), so $$\pi \times 12^2 - \pi \times 8^2 = 144\pi - 64\pi = 80\pi.$$ The area is \(80\pi\), so the whole number is \(\boxed{80}\).

blocks.x: The most famous relationship in all of geometry ties the three sides of a right triangle together, and it holds exactly, not just close enough. This lesson does more than hand you the formula. You will see why it has to be true using nothing but areas you already know, and then turn it loose to measure distances no ruler could ever reach.

In a right triangle, the two sides that meet at the right angle are the legs, and the side opposite the right angle is the hypotenuse — always the longest side, because it stretches across the widest corner. Build a square on each side: the Pythagorean Theorem says the square on the hypotenuse equals the two squares on the legs put together, $a^2+b^2=c^2$, with $a$ and $b$ the legs and $c$ the hypotenuse. For a 3-4-5 triangle, $9+16=25$.
In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(3\) and \(4\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The hypotenuse is the side opposite the right angle. The Pythagorean Theorem says the squares of the two legs add up to the square of the hypotenuse.
Square each leg and add, then take the square root of that total to get the hypotenuse.
blocks.sol: The two legs meet at the right angle, so their squares add to the square of the hypotenuse. By the theorem, \(c^2 = 3^2 + 4^2 = 9 + 16 = 25\). Take the square root, \(c = \sqrt{25} = 5\). The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{5}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(6\) and \(8\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The hypotenuse is the side across from the right angle. The Pythagorean Theorem says its square equals the sum of the squares of the two legs.
Square each leg and add them to get \(c^2\). Then take the square root to find \(c\).
blocks.sol: By the Pythagorean Theorem, the square of the hypotenuse is the sum of the squares of the legs. $$c^2 = 6^2 + 8^2 = 36 + 64 = 100$$ Take the square root, so \(c = \sqrt{100} = 10\). The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{10}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(5\) and \(12\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The hypotenuse \(c\) is the side opposite the right angle. By the Pythagorean Theorem, \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\), so square each leg and add.
You have \(c^2 = 5^2 + 12^2\). Work out the two squares, add them, then take the square root to get \(c\).
blocks.sol: The hypotenuse is opposite the right angle, so it is the \(c\) in the Pythagorean Theorem. Add the squares of the two legs. $$c^2 = 5^2 + 12^2 = 25 + 144 = 169.$$ Now take the square root. Since \(13^2 = 169\), we get \(c = \sqrt{169} = 13\). This is the 5-12-13 right triangle, one worth remembering. The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{13}\).

blocks.x: Here is a proof you can see. Fit four copies of the right triangle into a big square of side $a+b$, leaving a tilted square hole of side $c$ in the middle. The big square has area $(a+b)^2$; that same area is also the four triangles plus the hole, $4\cdot\frac{1}{2}ab + c^2 = 2ab + c^2$. Since $(a+b)^2 = a^2+2ab+b^2$, the $2ab$ sits on both sides and cancels, leaving $a^2+b^2=c^2$.
In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(13\) and one leg \(5\). The other leg is the question mark. Find the missing leg. Give the number of units.
hints: The Pythagorean Theorem says \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). Here the hypotenuse is known and one leg is missing, so rearrange to \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\) and subtract instead of add.
Compute \(13^2\) and \(5^2\), subtract, then take the square root of what is left.
blocks.sol: This time we know the hypotenuse and one leg, so we subtract instead of add. Put the missing leg on its own side of \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). $$b^2 = 13^2 - 5^2 = 169 - 25 = 144$$ Now take the square root, \(b = \sqrt{144} = 12\). This is the 5-12-13 triple, so the missing leg is \(\boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(12\) and \(16\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The Pythagorean Theorem says \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). Square both legs and add to get \(c^2\), then take the square root.
Look closer at \(12\) and \(16\). They are \(4\) times \(3\) and \(4\), so this is a 3-4-5 triangle scaled up by \(4\).
blocks.sol: By the Pythagorean Theorem, add the squares of the legs to get the square of the hypotenuse. $$12^2 + 16^2 = 144 + 256 = 400$$ So \(c = \sqrt{400} = 20\). You could also spot that \(12\) and \(16\) are \(4\) times \(3\) and \(4\). This is a 3-4-5 triangle scaled by \(4\), so the hypotenuse is \(4 \times 5 = 20\). The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{20}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(8\) and \(15\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The hypotenuse squared equals the sum of the squares of the legs, so \(c^2 = 8^2 + 15^2\).
Add \(64 + 225\), then take the square root of that total.
blocks.sol: By the Pythagorean Theorem, the square of the hypotenuse is the sum of the squares of the legs. $$c^2 = 8^2 + 15^2 = 64 + 225 = 289$$ Take the square root, \(c = \sqrt{289} = 17\). This is the 8-15-17 triple. The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{17}\).

blocks.x: The theorem works both ways. To find the hypotenuse, add the squares of the legs and take the root. To find a missing leg, subtract: $b^2 = c^2 - a^2$, since the hypotenuse gives the biggest square and everything comes out of it. If you ever add while hunting for a leg, you have the roles mixed up. A few right triangles have whole numbers on all three sides — 3-4-5, 5-12-13, 8-15-17 — and any multiple works too (6-8-10, 9-12-15). Spot a triple and you can read the answer off without arithmetic.
In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(17\) and one leg \(8\). Find the missing leg. Give the number of units.
hints: This time the hypotenuse is known and a leg is missing, so rearrange the Pythagorean Theorem to \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\).
Square the hypotenuse and the known leg, subtract, then take the square root of what is left.
blocks.sol: The hypotenuse is given and one leg is missing, so subtract instead of add. Square the hypotenuse and pull out the known leg. $$b^2 = 17^2 - 8^2 = 289 - 64 = 225$$ Take the square root, \(b = \sqrt{225} = 15\). The missing leg is \(\boxed{15}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, a ladder leans against a wall. Its foot sits \(7\) feet from the wall, and the top reaches \(24\) feet up the wall. How long is the ladder? Give the number of units.
hints: The wall and the ground meet at a right angle, so the ladder, the ground, and the wall form a right triangle. The ladder leans across from the right angle, so it is the hypotenuse. The two legs are the \(7\) feet along the ground and the \(24\) feet up the wall.
By the Pythagorean Theorem, square each leg and add them to get the square of the ladder's length, \(c^2 = 7^2 + 24^2\). Work that sum out, then take the square root to get \(c\).
blocks.sol: The ladder is the hypotenuse, the side reaching across from the right angle where the wall meets the ground. The legs are \(7\) and \(24\), so the Pythagorean Theorem gives $$c^2 = 7^2 + 24^2 = 49 + 576 = 625.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{625} = 25\). This is the \(7\)-\(24\)-\(25\) right triangle, another whole-number set worth recognizing on sight. The ladder is \(\boxed{25}\) feet long.

blocks.x: The real power of the theorem is a length no ruler could ever reach. A ladder against a wall, a wire between two poles, a straight path across a park — the slanted distance is always the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose two legs you can measure. Pace out one leg along the ground, look up the wall or pole for the other, then square them, add, and take the root. $a^2+b^2=c^2$ turns two easy measurements you can reach into one long one you never could.

In the diagram above, the tops of two poles are joined by a straight wire. The tops differ in height by \(20\) feet, and the poles stand \(21\) feet apart. Find the length of the wire. Give the number of units.
hints: The wire, the horizontal gap, and the height difference form a right triangle, with the wire as the hypotenuse. Which two lengths are the legs?
By the Pythagorean Theorem, square the two legs and add them, then take the square root to get the wire.
blocks.sol: The wire is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are the height difference \(20\) and the horizontal gap \(21\). By the Pythagorean Theorem, $$c^2 = 20^2 + 21^2 = 400 + 441 = 841.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{841} = \boxed{29}\). The wire is \(29\) feet long.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, two points sit \(9\) units apart horizontally and \(40\) units apart vertically. The straight line between them is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are those two gaps. Find the straight distance between the points. Give the number of units.
hints: The horizontal and vertical gaps are the two legs, and the straight distance is the hypotenuse \(c\). Use \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\) with the legs \(9\) and \(40\).
Square each leg and add. \(9^2 = 81\) and \(40^2 = 1600\), so \(c^2 = 1681\). Now take the square root.
blocks.sol: The straight distance is the hypotenuse, with the horizontal and vertical gaps as the legs. By the Pythagorean Theorem, $$c^2 = 9^2 + 40^2 = 81 + 1600 = 1681.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{1681} = 41\). So the distance is \(\boxed{41}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, a rectangle has a diagonal of length \(26\) and one side of length \(10\). Find the length of the other side. Give the number of units.
hints: The diagonal cuts the rectangle into two right triangles. In each one the two sides of the rectangle are the legs and the diagonal is the hypotenuse.
You know the hypotenuse and one leg, so use \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\) to find the missing leg.
blocks.sol: The diagonal is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are the two sides of the rectangle. To find a missing leg you subtract the squares and take the square root, $$\sqrt{26^2 - 10^2} = \sqrt{676 - 100} = \sqrt{576} = 24.$$ So the other side is \(\boxed{24}\).

blocks.x: Though it carries Pythagoras's name, this relationship was known to the Babylonians more than a thousand years before he was born. Clay tablets show they were already working with whole-number right triangles. Today there are over four hundred different proofs of the theorem, and one of them was worked out by James Garfield, a future president of the United States, while he was serving in Congress.
In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(35\) and \(12\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The Pythagorean Theorem says the squares of the two legs add up to the square of the hypotenuse, \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). Square each leg and add.
You should have \(35^2 + 12^2 = 1369\). The hypotenuse is the square root of that. Try to spot which whole number squares to \(1369\).
blocks.sol: By the Pythagorean Theorem, the squares of the legs add to the square of the hypotenuse. $$c^2 = 35^2 + 12^2 = 1225 + 144 = 1369$$ Take the square root, and \(c = \sqrt{1369} = 37\). This is the 12-35-37 right triangle. The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{37}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(25\) and one leg \(20\). The other leg is unknown. Find the area of the triangle. Give the number of square units.
hints: To find the area you need a base and a height, and the two legs work perfectly. But you only know one leg, so use the Pythagorean Theorem first to get the missing one.
Once you have both legs, the area of a right triangle is half the product of the legs, since the legs are the base and height.
blocks.sol: This is two steps. First find the missing leg with the Pythagorean Theorem. The hypotenuse is \(25\) and one leg is \(20\), so $$b = \sqrt{25^2 - 20^2} = \sqrt{625 - 400} = \sqrt{225} = 15.$$ Now the two legs are \(20\) and \(15\), and in a right triangle the legs are the base and height. So the area is $$\frac{1}{2}\times 20 \times 15 = \boxed{150}.$$

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(16\) and \(12\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The hypotenuse is the side opposite the right angle. By the Pythagorean Theorem it equals \(\sqrt{16^2 + 12^2}\), so square each leg, add, then take the square root.
review.sol: The two legs are \(16\) and \(12\), so the hypotenuse is the square root of the sum of their squares.$$c = \sqrt{16^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{256 + 144} = \sqrt{400} = 20$$So the hypotenuse is \(\boxed{20}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(24\) and \(7\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The hypotenuse is the longest side, opposite the right angle. By the Pythagorean Theorem its square equals the sum of the squares of the two legs, so square \(24\) and \(7\), add them, then take the square root.
review.sol: The two legs form the right angle, so they are \(a\) and \(b\), and the hypotenuse is \(c\). By the Pythagorean Theorem, $$c^2 = 24^2 + 7^2 = 576 + 49 = 625.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{625} = \boxed{25}\). This is the 7-24-25 right triangle, another whole-number triple worth recognizing on sight.

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(21\) and \(20\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The two shorter sides are the legs, so use \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). Square each leg, add, then take the square root.
review.sol: By the Pythagorean Theorem, the hypotenuse is the square root of the sum of the squares of the legs. $$c = \sqrt{21^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{441 + 400} = \sqrt{841}$$ Since \(29^2 = 841\), the hypotenuse is \(\boxed{29}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(25\) and one leg \(24\). Find the other leg. Give the number of units.
hints: The missing side is a leg, so the theorem rearranges to \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\). Subtract the square of the known leg from the square of the hypotenuse, then take the square root.
review.sol: The hypotenuse is the longest side, \(25\), and one leg is \(24\), so the missing leg \(b\) satisfies \(24^2 + b^2 = 25^2\). Subtracting to isolate \(b^2\),$$b^2 = 25^2 - 24^2 = 625 - 576 = 49.$$Take the square root, \(b = \sqrt{49} = \boxed{7}\). This is the 7-24-25 right triangle, one more of the whole-number triples worth recognizing on sight.

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(15\) and one leg \(9\). Find the missing leg. Give the number of units.
hints: You know the hypotenuse and one leg, so use the theorem in its subtraction form, \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\). Square each length, subtract, then take the square root.
review.sol: Since we know the hypotenuse and want a leg, we subtract. By the Pythagorean Theorem, \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\), so $$b^2 = 15^2 - 9^2 = 225 - 81 = 144.$$ Taking the square root, \(b = \sqrt{144} = 12\). The missing leg is \(\boxed{12}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(41\) and one leg \(40\). Find the length of the other leg. Give the number of units.
hints: You know the hypotenuse and one leg, so this is a subtract-then-take-the-square-root problem. \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\). Square both known sides and subtract.
review.sol: To find a missing leg, subtract the squares and take the square root. Here the hypotenuse is \(41\) and the known leg is \(40\), so$$b^2 = 41^2 - 40^2 = 1681 - 1600 = 81,$$and \(b = \sqrt{81} = 9\). The three sides \(9\), \(40\), \(41\) are a Pythagorean triple. The other leg is \(\boxed{9}\) units.

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(24\) and \(10\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The Pythagorean Theorem says \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\), so square both legs and add them, then take the square root to get the hypotenuse.
review.sol: The hypotenuse is the longest side, opposite the right angle. By the Pythagorean Theorem, \(c^2 = 24^2 + 10^2\), so $$c = \sqrt{576 + 100} = \sqrt{676} = 26.$$ The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{26}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, a ladder leans against a wall. Its foot sits \(5\) feet from the wall and its top reaches \(12\) feet up. The ladder itself is the hypotenuse of the right triangle. How long is the ladder? Give the number of units.
hints: The wall and the ground are the two legs, and the ladder is the hypotenuse. By the Pythagorean Theorem the ladder's length squared equals \(5^2 + 12^2\). Add those, then take the square root.
review.sol: The wall and the ground meet at a right angle, so the ladder is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs \(5\) and \(12\). By the Pythagorean Theorem, $$c^2 = 5^2 + 12^2 = 25 + 144 = 169.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{169} = 13\). This is the \(5\text{-}12\text{-}13\) right triangle, one of the whole-number triples worth knowing by heart. The ladder is \(\boxed{13}\) feet long.

review.x: In the diagram above the two points are \(8\) apart horizontally and \(15\) apart vertically. The straight line joining them is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are those two gaps. Find the distance between the points. Give the number of units.
hints: The horizontal and vertical gaps are the two legs of a right triangle, and the distance you want is the hypotenuse. By the Pythagorean Theorem, add the squares of the legs and take the square root.
review.sol: The horizontal gap and the vertical gap are the legs of a right triangle, and the distance between the points is the hypotenuse. By the Pythagorean Theorem, add the squares of the legs and take the square root. $$\sqrt{8^2 + 15^2} = \sqrt{64 + 225} = \sqrt{289} = 17$$ So the distance between the points is \(\boxed{17}\) units. This is the 8-15-17 triple, one worth recognizing on sight.

review.x: In the diagram above, a wire runs from the top of one pole to the top of another. The tops differ in height by \(24\) feet, and the poles stand \(18\) feet apart. Find the length of the wire. Give the number of units.
hints: The wire is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are the horizontal gap and the height difference. Use \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\).
review.sol: The wire is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs \(18\) and \(24\), so by the Pythagorean Theorem $$18^2 + 24^2 = 324 + 576 = 900.$$ Take the square root, \(\sqrt{900} = 30\). The wire is \(\boxed{30}\) feet long.

review.x: The diagram above shows a rectangle with a diagonal of \(37\) and one side of \(12\). The diagonal cuts the rectangle into a right triangle, with the diagonal as the hypotenuse. Find the length of the other side. Give the number of units.
hints: The diagonal is the hypotenuse and the side of \(12\) is one leg, so use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the missing leg: square the diagonal, subtract the square of the known side, then take the square root.
review.sol: The diagonal is the hypotenuse, so the missing side is a leg. To find a leg, subtract the squares. $$b^2 = 37^2 - 12^2 = 1369 - 144 = 1225.$$ Take the square root. \(b = \sqrt{1225} = 35\). The other side is \(\boxed{35}\) units.

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(40\) and \(9\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: To find the hypotenuse, the Pythagorean Theorem says to add the squares of the two legs and take the square root.
review.sol: The hypotenuse squared equals the sum of the squares of the legs, so $$c^2 = 40^2 + 9^2 = 1600 + 81 = 1681.$$ Take the square root, \(c = \sqrt{1681} = 41\). The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{41}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(17\) and one leg \(15\). Find the area of the triangle. Give the number of square units.
hints: The two legs are the base and height, so you need the missing leg first. Use \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\) to find it, then take half of base times height.
review.sol: You can't find the area yet, because you only have one leg. Get the other one from the theorem, subtracting to find a missing leg.$$b^2 = 17^2 - 15^2 = 289 - 225 = 64,\quad b = \sqrt{64} = 8$$Now the two legs, \(15\) and \(8\), are the base and height of the right triangle, so the area of a triangle is half of base times height.$$\tfrac{1}{2}\times 15 \times 8 = \boxed{60}$$The area is \(60\) square units.

blocks.x: Cut a square in half along its diagonal and you get a right triangle with two equal legs. Triangles like this appear everywhere, in ramps, rooftops, and folded napkins, so it pays to know them by heart. Start by measuring one with the tool you already own, the Pythagorean Theorem.
A right triangle has legs \(1\) and \(1\). Use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the square of the hypotenuse. What number is \(c^2\)?
hints: The theorem says \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\), and here both legs are \(1\).
blocks.sol: The legs give \(c^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2\), so the hypotenuse itself is \(\sqrt{2}\). The square of the hypotenuse is \(\boxed{2}\).

blocks.x: The 45-45-90 triangle. A right triangle with two equal legs also has two equal \(45\) degree angles, so we call it a 45-45-90 triangle. If each leg is \(s\), the Pythagorean Theorem gives \(c^2 = s^2 + s^2 = 2s^2\), so the hypotenuse is \(c = s\sqrt{2}\). Whatever the leg, the hypotenuse is that length times \(\sqrt{2}\), about \(1.41\) times longer. Every 45-45-90 triangle is half of a square, which is why the pattern never changes.
Each leg of a 45-45-90 triangle is \(5\). The hypotenuse simplifies to \(k\sqrt{2}\). What whole number is \(k\)?
hints: The hypotenuse of a 45-45-90 triangle is the leg times \(\sqrt{2}\).
With legs of \(5\), the hypotenuse is \(5\sqrt{2}\).
